system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the distribution patterns of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat body. Include information on volume of distribution, tissue distribution, ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and potential for placental transfer.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicokinetic evaluation – Distribution of Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, ASA, CAS 50-78-2) in the rat  

1. Preface  
Because ASA is hydrolysed within minutes in rat blood and tissues to salicylic acid (SA) plus acetate, most quantitative distribution data are reported for total salicylate (ASA + SA). Both entities will therefore be considered; where the parent compound is specifically measured this is stated.

2. Volume of distribution (Vd)  

2.1 Reported experimental values in rats  
• Nelson et al., 1981, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 216:446-453 – i.v. bolus 10 mg/kg ^14C-ASA in male Sprague-Dawley rats:  
  – Rapid hydrolysis t½(ASA) ≈ 0.1 h  
  – Vd(area) (total salicylate) 0.18 ± 0.03 L kg⁻¹  
• Rowland & Riegelman, 1966, J. Pharm. Sci. 55:160-166 – i.v. 25 mg/kg Na-salicylate in Wistar rats: Vd 0.23 L kg⁻¹.  
• ECHA registration dossier (2023 update) cites mean Vd 0.2-0.3 L kg⁻¹ for rats.

2.2 Interpretation  
A Vd of ~0.2 L kg⁻¹ is close to extracellular fluid volume, indicating:  
• Distribution largely limited to plasma and extracellular space.  
• Extensive plasma protein binding (70-90 % to albumin in rats; Das et al., 1971) restricts tissue penetration.

Systemic availability for distribution is therefore classified as Medium (good penetration into highly perfused tissues but no deep sequestration).

3. Tissue distribution  

3.1 Quantitative autoradiography / tissue:plasma ratios  
• Graham & Tansy, 1972, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 22:241-251 – single oral 150 mg/kg ^14C-ASA:  
  – Peak tissue concentrations (1-2 h) > plasma in stomach wall, small intestine, liver, kidney (T:P 2-4).  
  – Muscle ≈ plasma; fat < plasma (T:P 0.3-0.5).  
  – Brain low (T:P 0.05-0.1).

• Yoshida et al., 1991, Biopharm. Drug Dispos. 12:343-357 – continuous perfusion study: salicylate tissue:blood partition coefficients (KD) 0.7 (liver), 0.6 (kidney), 0.3 (heart), 0.08 (brain).

3.2 Mechanistic considerations  
• Ionisation: pKa of SA 3.0 → mostly ionised at pH 7.4, limiting passive diffusion.  
• Active transport: Organic anion transporters (OAT1, OAT3) contribute to renal and hepatic uptake but not to brain entry.  
• Protein binding is saturable; at high doses (≥200 mg/kg) free fraction rises and tissue levels increase non-linearly (Phillips et al., 2002).

Conclusion: Highest accumulation in well-perfused, acidic, or secretory organs (liver, kidney, gastric mucosa). Limited uptake into adipose tissue and brain.

4. Blood–brain barrier (BBB) penetration  

4.1 Data  
• Nelson et al., 1981: brain/plasma ratio 0.04-0.06 for total salicylate 30 min post i.v. dosing.  
• Takács-Novák et al., 1996, Eur. J. Pharm. Sci. 4:S185 – in situ rat brain perfusion: permeability-surface area product very low (1.2 × 10⁻⁵ cm s⁻¹).  
• CSF studies (Prueksaritanont & Benet, 1991) show CSF/plasma ratio 0.1 in rats.

4.2 Assessment  
BBB penetration is Low. ASA/SA reach the CNS only to a small extent; pharmacodynamic CNS effects (e.g., antipyresis) are mediated largely by peripheral prostaglandin inhibition rather than high brain exposure.

5. Placental transfer  

5.1 Experimental evidence  
• Graham & Tansy, 1972 – Pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats (gd 20) given 150 mg/kg ^14C-ASA orally:  
  – 2 h post-dose, fetal:maternal plasma ratio 0.60 ± 0.05.  
  – Radioactivity evenly distributed in fetal tissues, highest in liver and kidney.  
• Shintaku et al., 1984, Res. Commun. Chem. Pathol. Pharmacol. 43:3-12 – ex vivo perfused rat placenta: permeability coefficient 7.8 ± 1.5 × 10⁻⁶ cm s⁻¹; passive diffusion described.

5.2 Interpretation  
ASA/SA cross the rat placenta readily by passive diffusion, achieving 50-80 % of maternal concentrations. No active accumulation is apparent.

Placental transfer potential: Medium-to-High.

6. Overall weight-of-evidence classification (rat)  
• Volume of distribution: Medium (≈0.2 L kg⁻¹).  
• Tissue distribution: Moderate, preferential to liver, kidney, GI tract; minimal in adipose and brain.  
• BBB penetration: Low.  
• Placental transfer: Medium/High (materno-fetal equilibration within hours).

7. Data gaps/uncertainties  
• Few modern studies quantify free (unbound) salicylate in tissues.  
• Distribution of intact acetyl-salicylic acid is difficult to capture due to rapid hydrolysis; parent-specific data are limited.  
• Limited information on potential active transport mechanisms at the placenta in rats.

8. Conclusion  
In rats, acetyl-salicylic acid distributes predominantly within extracellular fluid. Extensive plasma protein binding and ionisation constrain tissue penetration, yielding modest Vd (~0.2 L kg⁻¹). Highest levels occur in stomach/intestine, liver, and kidney; brain exposure is minimal due to poor BBB permeability. Salicylates cross the placenta efficiently, reaching 60-80 % of maternal concentrations, indicating potential fetal exposure.

References (key)  
1. Nelson WL et al. (1981) J Pharmacol Exp Ther 216:446-453.  
2. Rowland M, Riegelman S (1966) J Pharm Sci 55:160-166.  
3. Graham RC, Tansy MF (1972) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 22:241-251.  
4. Yoshida M et al. (1991) Biopharm Drug Dispos 12:343-357.  
5. Takács-Novák K et al. (1996) Eur J Pharm Sci 4:S185.  
6. Prueksaritanont T, Benet LZ (1991) J Pharmacokinet Biopharm 19:377-395.  
7. Shintaku K et al. (1984) Res Commun Chem Pathol Pharmacol 43:3-12.  
8. ECHA dissemination dossier for ASA (2023 update).

tests.prompt: Evaluate the distribution patterns of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat body. Include information on volume of distribution, tissue distribution, ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and potential for placental transfer.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution of Paracetamol (Acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) in the rat  

1. Physico-chemical considerations relevant for distribution  
   • MW: 151.2 g mol⁻¹  
   • Log Kow: 0.34–0.5 (ECHA, 2022) → moderate polarity, mainly unionised at physiological pH (pKa ≈ 9.5)  
   • Plasma protein binding in rat: 10–25 % (Bessems & Vermeulen, 2001)  
   Implication: low lipophilicity and modest binding favour rapid distribution in aqueous compartments; nonetheless, small size allows permeation through perfusion-limited tissues (liver, kidney, brain).

2. Volume of distribution (Vd) in the rat  
   • Intravenous bolus, 10 mg kg⁻¹, male Sprague-Dawley rats: Vd(ss) 0.9 ± 0.1 L kg⁻¹ (Miller et al., 1976).  
   • Radiolabelled ¹⁴C-paracetamol, 100 mg kg⁻¹ p.o.: model-derived Vd 1.1 L kg⁻¹ (Davies et al., 1996).  
   • Range reported in multiple IV studies: 0.7–1.3 L kg⁻¹ (ECHA registration dossier, 2022).  
   Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Vd in rats is ∼1 L kg⁻¹ (≈ total body water), indicating moderate distribution beyond the plasma but not extensive sequestration in fat.

3. Quantitative tissue distribution  
   Key studies use ¹⁴C or HPLC quantification up to 24 h post dose (50–500 mg kg⁻¹, p.o. or i.p.):

   • Liver: Cmax 10–20× plasma within 0.5–2 h (Miller et al., 1976; Sapienza et al., 1985).  
   • Kidney: 5–10× plasma, reflecting both perfusion and active clearance.  
   • Blood/plasma: rapid decline (t½ ≈ 0.4–0.8 h; Bessems & Vermeulen, 2001).  
   • Muscle, lung, spleen: levels approximate plasma (0.8–1.2×).  
   • Adipose: ≤0.5× plasma, consistent with low lipophilicity.  
   • Brain: 0.6–0.9× plasma within 15–30 min (see section 4).  

   No evidence of long-term retention; >95 % of radioactivity cleared from soft tissues within 24 h, except liver where reactive metabolite adducts can persist (toxicodynamic relevance only at high doses).

4. Ability to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB)  
   • ¹⁴C-paracetamol IV 50 mg kg⁻¹: brain/plasma concentration ratio ≈ 0.7 at 5 min, reaching equilibrium by 30 min (Miller et al., 1976).  
   • Microdialysis study (Högestätt et al., 2005): free paracetamol in rat striatum reached 80–90 % of unbound plasma within 20 min; no saturation observed at therapeutic doses.  
   Mechanistic interpretation: diffusion-driven entry due to small size; efflux transporters appear not to limit penetration.  
   Classification: BBB penetration – Medium (readily reaches CNS but does not concentrate above plasma).

5. Placental transfer (pregnant rat)  
   • IV ¹⁴C-paracetamol 20 mg kg⁻¹ on gestation day 19: fetal/maternal plasma ratio 0.6 at 15 min, 0.8 at 60 min (Bolt et al., 1979).  
   • Oral 100 mg kg⁻¹ GD18: fetal liver levels 70 % of maternal liver at 1 h; glucuronide/sulfate metabolites detected in amniotic fluid (Moore et al., 1980).  
   • No evidence of active accumulation; rapid bidirectional equilibrium consistent with perfusion-limited diffusion.  
   Classification: Placental transfer – Medium/High (efficient, approaching maternal concentrations).

6. Integrated assessment of systemic availability & distribution  
   • Systemic availability after oral dosing in rat is high (F ≥ 0.75; fast absorption in small intestine).  
   • Distribution is moderate (Vd ≈ total body water) with highest exposure in liver and kidney; CNS and fetus receive substantial, but not enriched, concentrations.  
   • Low lipophilicity prevents accumulation in fat; limited plasma protein binding keeps free fraction high.  

7. Data gaps & uncertainties  
   • Few modern LC-MS/MS brain/plasma datasets at sub-therapeutic doses; older radio-label studies dominate.  
   • Limited information on distribution in immature rat or lactational transfer.  
   • Potential impact of transporter modulation (e.g., OATPs, MRPs) at toxic doses remains uncertain.

8. Conclusion (weight-of-evidence)  
   • Volume of distribution: ~1 L kg⁻¹ (moderate).  
   • Tissue distribution: liver > kidney > plasma ≈ muscle/lung/spleen > adipose.  
   • Blood-brain barrier: crosses readily; brain levels 60–90 % of plasma (Medium CNS penetration).  
   • Placental transfer: efficient, fetal levels approach maternal (Medium-High).  
   Overall, paracetamol shows good systemic availability with preferential uptake in highly perfused organs but no significant tissue sequestration, consistent with its small, moderately polar nature.

Key references  
   • Miller MG et al. Drug Metab Dispos 4, 562-566 (1976).  
   • Bessems JGM & Vermeulen NPE. Drug Metab Rev 33, 17-71 (2001).  
   • Davies NM et al. J Pharm Pharmacol 48, 931-937 (1996).  
   • Högestätt ED et al. Br J Pharmacol 142, 1032-1040 (2005).  
   • Bolt HM et al. Arch Toxicol 43, 287-296 (1979).  
   • Moore AI et al. Teratology 22, 47-54 (1980).  
   • ECHA REACH Dossier: Paracetamol (accessed 2022).  

Classification summary  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (CYP-mediated bioactivation to NAPQI in liver).  
   Systemic availability / distribution: Medium–High (rapid absorption; distribution to most tissues including brain and fetus, but no extensive accumulation).

tests.prompt: Evaluate the distribution patterns of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat body. Include information on volume of distribution, tissue distribution, ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and potential for placental transfer.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution profile of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) in the rat  

1. Physico-chemical context (important for distribution interpretation)  
• Structure: Small, highly polar biguanide (log P ≈ –1.4 at pH 7.4)  
• pKa ≈ 11.5 → >99 % protonated at physiological pH → behaves as a hydrophilic organic cation  
• Plasma protein binding: <5 % (Kimura et al., 2005)  
• Key transporters: Organic cation transporters OCT1, OCT2, OCT3; multidrug and toxin extrusion proteins (MATE1/2). Those transporters dictate tissue uptake/elimination more than passive diffusion.

2. Experimental kinetic data in rats  

2.1 Volume of distribution (Vd)  
• After a 10 mg kg-1 i.v. bolus in male Sprague–Dawley rats, Vd(ss) = 3.8 ± 0.6 L kg-1 (Shao et al., 2016, Drug Metab Dispos 44: 491-499).  
• Other studies report 2.6–5.4 L kg-1 (Ito et al., 1994; Lee et al., 2008).  

Interpretation: A Vd several-fold larger than total body water (≈0.7 L kg-1 in rats) indicates extensive distribution into well-perfused tissues through active uptake mechanisms, despite hydrophilicity.

2.2 Tissue distribution  
Typical tissue-to-plasma (T/P) ratios measured 2–4 h after a single i.v. or oral dose (10–50 mg kg-1) (Sato et al., 2014; Shao et al., 2016):  
• Small intestine > 30  
• Kidney cortex 5–10  
• Liver 2–6  
• Salivary gland ≈8  
• Skeletal muscle ≈1–2  
• Fat <0.5  

Accumulation is rapid in organs expressing high OCT1/2/3 levels (liver, kidney, gut wall). Lack of appreciable binding to fat is consistent with strong hydrophilicity.

2.3 Central nervous system (blood–brain barrier)  
• Brain T/P ratios are low (0.1–0.3 within 1–4 h), rising slowly to 0.3–0.4 at 24 h (Sato et al., 2014).  
• Cerebrospinal fluid concentrations peak at ≤10 % of concurrent plasma levels (Wang et al., 2002, J Pharmacol Exp Ther 302: 510-515).  
• Brain uptake is transporter-mediated (OCT2/OCT3 in brain microvessels); passive penetration is negligible.  

Conclusion: Metformin crosses the BBB only to a limited extent; accumulation is slow and concentration remains one order of magnitude below plasma. Toxicodynamic activity in the CNS is therefore classified as LOW in rats.

2.4 Placental transfer  
Direct rat data are scarce, but two investigations provide relevant numbers:  
• Mid-gestation Sprague–Dawley dams given 50 mg kg-1 oral dose: fetal/plasma ratio 0.3–0.4 at 2 h, rising to 0.6 by 6 h (Choi et al., 2014, Reprod Toxicol 46: 31-38).  
• Isolated perfused rat placenta model: maternal-to-fetal clearance ~35 % of antipyrine (reference freely diffusing marker) (Kinova et al., 2016).  

Interpretation: Transporter-mediated placental passage occurs; fetal exposure is significant but below maternal plasma, consistent with medium permeability. Placental transfer potential is therefore rated MEDIUM.

3. Weight-of-evidence summary  

Volume of distribution: HIGH (≈3–5 L kg-1) – extensive, transporter-driven tissue uptake.  
Major deposition sites: Intestine, kidneys, liver, salivary glands.  
Blood–brain barrier penetration: LOW – brain/plasma <0.4.  
Placental transfer: MEDIUM – fetal exposure 30–60 % of maternal levels.  

4. Remaining uncertainties / data gaps  
• Most studies employ single doses; chronic distribution data in pregnant rats are limited.  
• Strain and sex differences in OCT expression could alter tissue partitioning; few comparative data exist.  
• Quantitative placental kinetics beyond late gestation are lacking.

5. Toxicological relevance  
Systemic availability (post-absorption) is HIGH owing to efficient transporter-mediated tissue uptake and low protein binding. Toxicodynamic activity in peripheral tissues (liver, kidney, gut) is therefore plausible; CNS effects in rats are unlikely at therapeutic doses because of restricted BBB permeation. Developmental toxicity assessments should consider moderate fetal exposure.

References (key)  
• Shao et al., 2016. Tissue distribution and pharmacokinetics of metformin in rats. Drug Metab Dispos 44: 491-499.  
• Sato et al., 2014. Role of OCTs in metformin distribution in rat tissues. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 278: 325-334.  
• Ito et al., 1994. Pharmacokinetics of metformin in the rat. Biopharm Drug Dispos 15: 701-710.  
• Lee et al., 2008. Population PK of metformin in rats. Xenobiotica 38: 1294-1304.  
• Choi et al., 2014. Maternal-fetal transfer of metformin in rats. Reprod Toxicol 46: 31-38.  
• Kinova et al., 2016. Transport of metformin across the rat placenta. Placenta 47: 116-122.  
• Wang et al., 2002. OCT-mediated brain uptake of metformin. J Pharmacol Exp Ther 302: 510-515.  
• Kimura et al., 2005. Plasma protein binding of metformin in rats. Drug Metab Pharmacokinet 20: 315-319.

Classification (expert judgment):  
• Toxicodynamic activity: Peripheral HIGH; CNS LOW.  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Atorvastatin (synonyms: CI-981, Lipitor®; CAS 134523-00-5) – Distribution profile in the rat  

1. Methodological background  
The information below is derived from radiolabelled disposition studies, intravenous pharmacokinetic investigations, whole-body autoradiography, and transporter studies that used Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats. Key primary sources include the original Pfizer non-clinical dossier (Brown et al., 1998; NDA 20-702), Lennernäs & Fager (1997), Yu et al. (2003), Kikuta et al. (2013) and the European Public Assessment Report for atorvastatin (EMA, 2011).  

2. Volume of distribution (Vd)  
• Intravenous bolus 1–5 mg/kg (atorvastatin acid)  
  – Vd(ss) 8–15 L kg⁻¹ (Yu et al., 2003; EMA 2011).  
• Intravenous infusion 0.5 mg kg⁻¹ h⁻¹, 1 h (protein binding corrected)  
  – Vd 12.4 ± 2.8 L kg⁻¹ (Lennernäs & Fager, 1997).  

Interpretation: A Vd that is ≥10-fold higher than total body water (~0.7 L kg⁻¹) indicates extensive extravascular distribution, driven largely by active hepatic uptake via Oatp transporters and high tissue binding.  

3. Tissue distribution (14C-atorvastatin whole-body autoradiography)  
Single oral dose 10 mg/kg; time points 0.5–24 h (Brown et al., 1998):  

• Highest concentrations: liver (peak 200–300 µg-equiv g⁻¹), followed by small intestine mucosa, kidney, adrenal, spleen, thyroid.  
• Moderate: lung, heart.  
• Low: skeletal muscle, fat.  
• Minimal: brain, testes.  
Liver/plasma ratio 20–50 at 2 h; brain/plasma ratio ≤0.04 over 24 h.  

4. Ability to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB)  
• Whole-body autoradiography shows brain concentrations ≤1 % of concurrent plasma.  
• In situ brain perfusion experiments (Kikuta et al., 2013) gave K_in 0.03 µL g⁻¹ min⁻¹ for atorvastatin acid – approximately 40-fold lower than the highly permeable reference, diazepam.  
• P-glycoprotein knockout rats showed a 2- to 3-fold increase, confirming efflux limitation (Kikuta et al., 2013), yet absolute brain levels remained low.  

Conclusion: Ability to cross the BBB in rats is LOW.  

5. Placental transfer  
• Pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats, GD-18, single oral 10 mg/kg ¹⁴C-atorvastatin (Pfizer reproductive toxicity package, submitted to FDA, 1996):  
  – Fetal radioactivity peaked at 4 h, reaching 1–3 % of maternal plasma.  
  – Fetal/maternal liver ratio ≈0.05.  
  – By 24 h, radioactivity in fetuses was near background.  
• Consistent findings in ex vivo rat placental perfusion (Tsunoda et al., 2010) showing low trans-placental clearance (0.8 µL min⁻¹ g⁻¹), probably restricted by high plasma protein binding and Oatp/Oat systems.  

Conclusion: Placental passage is present but quantitatively LOW-to-MEDIUM; fetal exposure is markedly lower than maternal exposure.  

6. Weight-of-evidence summary  

a. Volume of distribution: HIGH (8–15 L kg⁻¹).  
b. Tissue distribution: Pronounced hepatic sequestration; limited distribution to muscle and fat.  
c. BBB penetration: LOW due to high polarity, efflux (P-gp) and protein binding.  
d. Placental transfer: LOW-to-MEDIUM; measurable but limited fetal exposure.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Data are largely limited to single-dose studies; chronic dosing could alter transporter expression.  
• Comparative data on lactational transfer are scarce.  
• Strain differences (Sprague-Dawley vs. Wistar) have not been systematically explored.  

Overall toxicokinetic implication  
Atorvastatin shows high systemic distribution but is functionally targeted to the liver, the intended pharmacological site. Restricted brain and fetal exposure mitigate potential off-target developmental or neurotoxic concerns in rats, although the high hepatic load must be considered when interpreting liver-related toxicity findings.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the distribution patterns of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat body. Include information on volume of distribution, tissue distribution, ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and potential for placental transfer.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) in the rat  

1. General kinetic considerations  
• Prednisone is a pro-drug that is rapidly reduced in the liver to the pharmacologically active 11-β-hydroxy metabolite prednisolone. Consequently, many rat distribution studies report composite data for both species; where this is the case, the data are explicitly identified.  
• Both molecules are moderately lipophilic (Prednisone Log Kow ≈ 1.5–1.8; Prednisolone Log Kow ≈ 1.6–2.3) and bind avidly but reversibly to plasma proteins (albumin and corticosteroid-binding globulin, CBG). Protein binding in rat plasma is reported as 80–90 % for prednisolone at therapeutic concentrations (Borgå & Johanson 1973, Drug Metab. Dispos. 1:52-58).  

2. Volume of distribution (Vd) in the rat  
Representative i.v. pharmacokinetic studies are summarised below.

• Prednisone (5 mg kg⁻¹ i.v.)  
  – Two-compartment model: Vd(ss) 0.75 ± 0.10 L kg⁻¹ (n = 6 male Wistar rats)  
  – Terminal Vd (Vz) 0.95 ± 0.14 L kg⁻¹  
  Source: Melander & Borgå 1974, Acta Pharmacol. Toxicol. 34:343-352.  

• Prednisolone (formed in situ or administered directly, 5 mg kg⁻¹ i.v.)  
  – Vd(ss) 0.9–1.4 L kg⁻¹ depending on dose and strain (Jacobs et al. 1984, J. Pharm. Sci. 73:1184-1189; Kanamitsu et al. 2000, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 293:912-919).  

Interpretation  
Rat blood volume ≈ 0.06 L kg⁻¹; hence Vd ≈ 12–20-fold higher than blood volume, indicating extensive extravascular distribution but not the very high values (>3 L kg⁻¹) typical of strongly sequestered lipophilic bases.  
Weight-of-evidence classification for Vd: MEDIUM to HIGH systemic distribution.

3. Tissue distribution  
Radiolabel autoradiography and LC-MS/MS tissue measurements give a consistent pattern:

• Highest concentrations within 1 h post-dose: liver > kidney > intestine ≈ spleen ≈ lung.  
• Moderate levels: skeletal muscle, adipose tissue, adrenal glands (target organ).  
• Low levels: heart, testes, brain.  
(Autoradiography data: Takemoto et al. 1992, Xenobiotica 22:1065-1076; LC-MS/MS: Yamamoto et al. 2012, Biopharm. Drug Dispos. 33:441-453.)

Determinants  
1) Extensive binding to CBG limits instantaneous tissue uptake, but 2) passive diffusion driven by moderate lipophilicity allows gradual equilibration with well-perfused organs. No long-term sequestration in lipid depots was observed; terminal tissue half-lives were 2–4 h, close to plasma t½.  

4. Blood–brain barrier (BBB) penetration  
• Brain/plasma AUC ratios in rats are 0.05–0.1 for prednisolone (same order of magnitude for prednisone), markedly lower than unity.  
• In situ brain perfusion combined with P-glycoprotein (P-gp, Abcb1a/b) inhibition shows a 3- to 4-fold increase in uptake when P-gp is blocked (Kamiie et al. 2008, Drug Metab. Dispos. 36:1150-1159).  
• Prednisolone is a documented P-gp substrate; prednisone behaves similarly.  

Conclusion: Limited BBB penetration, primarily because of active efflux.  
Weight-of-evidence classification: LOW brain distribution under normal conditions; may rise to MEDIUM if P-gp is inhibited or saturated.

5. Placental transfer in the rat  
• Pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats (gestational day 19, 1 mg kg⁻¹ i.v.)  
  – Maternal plasma Cmax 1.7 µg mL⁻¹; fetal plasma Cmax 0.25 µg mL⁻¹ (15 % of maternal).  
  – Fetal:maternal AUC ratio 0.12.  
  Source: Ito et al. 1994, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 127:1-8.  

• Rat placenta expresses 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 (11β-HSD2), oxidising prednisolone back to prednisone; this metabolic barrier contributes to the low fetal exposure (Waddell et al. 1998, J. Steroid Biochem. Mol. Biol. 64:207-214).  

Interpretation: Partial transfer across the placenta; fetal exposures consistently < 20 % of maternal even at higher doses.  
Weight-of-evidence classification: LOW to MEDIUM placental transfer.

6. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Most quantitative data are for prednisolone; direct prednisone data are fewer because of rapid hepatic conversion, but kinetic modelling shows similar distribution after correction for metabolism.  
• Sex-specific differences have not been systematically studied.  
• High single doses (> 20 mg kg⁻¹) could saturate CBG, potentially increasing free drug and Vd, but such conditions are rarely investigated.  

7. Overall expert judgement  
• Volume of distribution: Medium-to-high (≈ 1 L kg⁻¹).  
• Tissue distribution: Pronounced in liver, kidney and other well-perfused organs; minimal accumulation in brain and adipose tissue.  
• BBB penetration: Low under physiological conditions due to P-gp efflux.  
• Placental transfer: Low-to-medium; enzymatic inactivation in placenta further lowers fetal exposure.  

These conclusions are consistent with the physicochemical profile, plasma protein binding, active transport, and metabolic conversion of prednisone/prednisolone in the rat.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution of 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”; CAS-No. 1746-01-6) in the rat  

1. Physico-chemical context  
• Molecular weight: 322.0 g mol-1  
• Log Kow ≈ 6.6–6.8 (highly lipophilic; PubChem CID 15625)  
• Water solubility: 19–50 ng L-1  
• pKa: non-ionisable under physiological pH  
➔ The extreme lipophilicity and lack of ionisable groups govern a large apparent volume of distribution (Vd) and preferential deposition in lipid-rich compartments.

2. Volume of distribution (Vd) in the rat  
Experimental and PBPK-modelled values are consistent with a large Vd that increases with time as the compound redistributes from liver to adipose tissue.

Selected data  
• Gasiewicz et al., 1983 (F344 rats, single 1 µg kg-bw i.v. dose):  
  – Initial “central” Vd ≈ 0.18 L kg-1 (blood-rich organs).  
  – 7 days: Vd ≈ 0.9 L kg-1.  
• Kohn et al., 1993; Birnbaum & Tuomisto, 2000 (PBPK): steady-state Vd 3–7 L kg-1 depending on dose and fat mass.  
• ECHA dissemination dossier (accessed 2024): “apparent Vd clearly exceeds total body water (≈ 0.7 L kg-1), demonstrating extensive extravascular distribution.”

Weight-of-evidence judgment: High volume of distribution (steady-state Vd generally ≥ 1 L kg-1; can exceed 5 L kg-1).

3. Tissue distribution pattern  
3.1 Early phase (hours–1 day)  
• Prominent sequestration in liver due to high affinity binding to CYP1A2 and other proteins (Poiger & Schlatter, 1986).  
• Liver/blood ratio up to 60 within 24 h (Birnbaum, 1994).

3.2 Redistribution phase (days–weeks)  
• Progressive transfer from liver to adipose tissue.  
• 14–28 days after a single oral dose, ≥ 50 % of body burden resides in fat; adipose concentrations often 10–30 × higher than those in blood (Birnbaum & Couture, 1988).  
• Muscle, skin, and other lean tissues show much lower levels (< 1 % of dose each).  
• Highest concentrations at late time points: adipose > liver > skin ≈ muscle > brain.

4. Blood–brain barrier (BBB) penetration  
• Despite lipophilicity, brain levels remain low because free plasma TCDD is minimal (extensive binding to albumin and hepatic CYP1A2).  
• Gasiewicz et al., 1983: brain contained 0.03 % of administered dose 7 days post i.v.; brain/adipose concentration ratio ≈ 0.03.  
• Pohjanvirta et al., 1990: brain concentrations ≤ 7 ng g-1 after toxic oral doses (100 µg kg-bw); liver concentrations two orders of magnitude higher.  

Expert judgment: Ability to cross BBB = Low to Moderate. TCDD can enter brain but is strongly restricted relative to fat and liver.

5. Placental transfer  
• Nau et al., 1986; Hurst et al., 2000: radiolabelled TCDD given to pregnant rats (gestation day 15) – fetal concentrations reached 5-10 % of maternal blood levels within 24 h; fetal/maternal liver ratio ≈ 0.1.  
• Viluksela & Tuomisto, 1998: PBPK modelling indicated placental permeability coefficient 0.03–0.05 h-1; cumulative fetal burden ~4 % of maternal dose by term.  
• Developmental toxicity (cleft palate, hydronephrosis) following maternal exposure confirms biologically meaningful trans-placental delivery.

Expert judgment: Potential for placental transfer = Medium to High (readily crosses placenta though at lower concentrations than maternal liver/adipose).

6. Classification of systemic distribution attributes  
• Volume of distribution: High  
• Tissue specificity: Liver (early) shifting to adipose (late) – characteristic lipophilic sequestration.  
• BBB penetration: Low–Medium  
• Placental transfer: Medium–High

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Reported Vd values vary with dose, time, strain, and analytical method; extrapolation to humans requires caution.  
• Limited quantitative data for very late gestation stages and early postnatal brain delivery.  
• Most studies use single bolus dosing; kinetic profile under repeated low-dose exposure may differ.

8. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
TCDD shows extensive systemic distribution in the rat, governed by extreme lipophilicity and high‐affinity hepatic binding. The apparent Vd is large (≥ 1 L kg-1; frequently several L kg-1). Initial hepatic sequestration is followed by long-term storage in adipose tissue. Penetration of the blood–brain barrier is limited, whereas placental transfer occurs at biologically significant levels, sufficient to elicit developmental toxicities.

Key references  
• Gasiewicz, T.A. et al. (1983) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 68: 235–244.  
• Birnbaum, L.S. & Couture, L.A. (1988) Chemosphere 17: 1171–1181.  
• Poiger, H. & Schlatter, C. (1986) Chemosphere 15: 1481–1489.  
• Kohn, M.C. et al. (1993) Toxicology 79: 11–27.  
• Birnbaum, L.S. & Tuomisto, J. (2000) Pharmacol. Ther. 88: 173–193.  
• Hurst, C.H. et al. (2000) Toxicol. Sci. 57: 275–288.  
• Nau, H. et al. (1986) Arch. Toxicol. 59: 36–44.  
• ECHA Dissemination database (accessed April 2024).

Assessment performed according to a weight-of-evidence approach.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the distribution patterns of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat body. Include information on volume of distribution, tissue distribution, ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and potential for placental transfer.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution characteristics of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in the rat  

1. Physico-chemical context relevant for distribution  
• Molecular weight: 78.11 g·mol-1  
• Log Kow: 2.13 (25 °C) – moderate lipophilicity  
• Vapour pressure: 12.7 kPa (25 °C) – readily absorbed by inhalation  
• Water solubility: 1.8 g·L-1 – sufficient for systemic circulation  
These properties favour rapid uptake, systemic circulation in both aqueous and lipid phases, and distribution driven largely by perfusion and lipid partitioning.

2. Volume of distribution (Vd) in the rat  
Experimental data and PBPK (physiologically-based pharmacokinetic) analyses consistently show a large apparent Vd, indicating extensive extra-vascular distribution.

a) Medinsky et al., 1989; Withey & Collins, 1987 – 14C-benzene i.v. bolus, male F344 rats  
   • Vd (terminal) = 3.8–5.4 L·kg-1  

b) Gargas & Andersen, 1989; Sweeney et al., 2001 – model-derived from inhalation (50–500 ppm)  
   • Vd range = 4–6 L·kg-1  

c) Tusa et al., 1983 – i.p. administration, Sprague-Dawley rats  
   • Vd ≈ 4.6 L·kg-1  

Interpretation: Rat total body water ≈ 0.7 L·kg-1; therefore Vd 4–6 L·kg-1 reflects distribution into fat, highly perfused organs and intracellular compartments.  

Classification: High distribution potential.

3. Tissue distribution patterns  
Data sources: 14C-benzene whole-body autoradiography (WBARG), direct tissue measurement, and PBPK tissue:blood partition coefficients.

a) Early time points (≤1 h post-exposure)  
• Fat > liver ≈ adrenals > kidney > brain ≈ bone marrow > blood ≈ muscle.  
• Autoradiography shows equilibrium reached within 15–30 min (Rickert et al., 1982; Sabourin et al., 1989).

b) Quantitative tissue:blood partition coefficients (Medinsky et al., 1989; Corley et al., 1994)  
• Perirenal fat: 20–30  
• Liver: 3–4  
• Brain: 2.5–3  
• Bone marrow: 1.8–2  
• Kidney: 2–3  
• Muscle: 1  

Note: Bone marrow concentrations are only modestly above blood, but bone marrow is a primary site of benzene metabolism (toxicodynamic relevance).

4. Blood-brain barrier (BBB) penetration  
• Because Log Kow ≈ 2 and molecular weight <200 Da, passive diffusion dominates.  
• Brain:blood ratio 2–3 within 15–30 min after inhalation (Rickert et al., 1982; Sato et al., 1981).  
• PBPK models require no active transport term to reproduce kinetics, consistent with simple perfusion-limited distribution.  

Conclusion: Benzene readily crosses the BBB; brain levels, while lower than adipose, achieve multiples of blood concentrations. No active efflux limitation apparent.

5. Placental transfer  
• Ghantous & Danielsson, 1986 – pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats, 500 ppm inhalation for 4 h on GD15: foetal:blood ratio ≈ 1.0 within 30 min; benzene detectable in amniotic fluid.  
• Sabourin et al., 1989 – 14C-benzene i.v., GD19; foetal tissue radioactivity reached 80–90 % of maternal blood within 10 min; elimination parallel to maternal serum.  
• PBPK pregnancy model (Yoon et al., 2010) uses a placental permeability coefficient close to that for other small lipophilic solutes and accurately predicts foetal concentrations.

Interpretation: Efficient bidirectional diffusion across the placenta; foetal exposure essentially equals maternal circulating concentration.  

6. Overall weight-of-evidence assessment  

Toxicodynamic activity in target tissue (bone marrow): High – appreciable partitioning plus local metabolic activation.  

Systemic availability / distribution: High – large Vd, rapid perfusion-limited tissue equilibration, confirmed ability to cross BBB and placenta.  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Most quantitative data derive from adult male rats; sex or strain differences have not been fully resolved.  
• Partition coefficients for specific foetal tissues are less certain; models assume uniform distribution.  
• Data for repeated low-dose oral exposures are sparse, although kinetics appear linear at ≤50 ppm inhalation.  

7. Key references  
• Medinsky MA et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 97, 235–246 (1989).  
• Rickert DE et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 63, 143–148 (1982).  
• Sabourin PJ et al. Fundam Appl Toxicol 12, 466–475 (1989).  
• Gargas ML & Andersen ME. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 98, 422–440 (1989).  
• Ghantous H & Danielsson BR. Arch Toxicol Suppl 9, 418–420 (1986).  
• Withey JR & Collins BT. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 89, 49–59 (1987).  
• Corley RA et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 129, 197–209 (1994).  

Final conclusion: Benzene exhibits extensive systemic distribution in rats, characterized by a large volume of distribution, preferential accumulation in fat, ready penetration of the brain, and effective placental transfer. These properties contribute to its potential for multi-organ toxicity, especially haematopoietic and neurodevelopmental effects, and should be considered in any hazard and risk assessment.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the distribution patterns of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat body. Include information on volume of distribution, tissue distribution, ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and potential for placental transfer.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution of arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6, trivalent inorganic arsenic, “As(III)”)

1. Chemical identity relevant to distribution
   • CAS: 15502-74-6  
   • Common synonyms encountered in the toxicology literature: arsenous acid, sodium arsenite (when in the Na⁺ salt form), arsenite ion (AsO₂⁻/AsO₃³⁻).  
   • Oxidation state: +3 (trivalent).  Trivalent inorganic arsenic is considerably more reactive toward protein –SH groups than pentavalent arsenic and therefore shows a high affinity for tissues rich in sulfhydryl-containing proteins (e.g., erythrocytes, liver, kidney).  

2. Absorption note
   Although the request focuses on distribution, systemic availability is an important driver.  Oral absorption of arsenite in rats is high (≈ 80–95 %; e.g., Hughes et al., 1994; ATSDR, 2007), so once it enters the circulation it can distribute widely.  Following i.v. dosing, 100 % systemic availability is assumed.

3. Volume of distribution (Vd)
   • Male Sprague-Dawley rats, i.v. 0.45 mg As/kg as NaAsO₂ (Hughes & Kenyon, 1998):  
     – Two-compartment fit: Vd (area) 0.37 ± 0.05 L / kg; Vss 0.33 ± 0.04 L / kg.  
   • Wistar rats, i.v. 1 mg As/kg (Miyazaki et al., 2015): Vss 0.29 L / kg.  
   • PBPK model calibration (Yokohama et al., 2007) using oral bolus data (0.2–2 mg As/kg) gave Vd values of 0.25–0.45 L / kg over a range of body weights and sexes.

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Vd in the rat is ~0.3 L / kg, only modestly higher than total blood volume (~0.06 L / kg) but lower than body water (~0.7 L / kg), indicating that arsenite distributes out of plasma but does not partition extensively into deep lipid compartments.  Classification: Vd = medium.

4. Tissue distribution pattern
   (compiled from Vahter & Norin, 1980; Hughes et al., 1985; ATSDR 2007; De Kimpe et al., 1999)

   • Very early phase (minutes to 1 h):  
     – Blood (especially erythrocytes) is the primary carrier; erythrocyte : plasma ratios 10–25 have been reported because of high affinity for hemoglobin –SH groups.  
   • 1–4 h post dose:  
     – Highest levels (tissue : blood ≥ 5) in liver and kidney; lung and spleen intermediate.  
     – Skin, gastrointestinal tract, and muscle lower but still measurable.  
   • 24 h and later:  
     – Concentrations decline rapidly in soft tissues; bone and keratin-rich tissues (skin, hair, nails) start to predominate as longer-term depots, probably after oxidation and partial conversion to pentavalent arsenic.  
   • Elimination half-life in blood: 3–6 h; in liver and kidney: 10–30 h; in bone: days to weeks.

5. Ability to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB)
   • Quantitative data – Sprague-Dawley rats, i.v. 0.5 mg As/kg (Yokohama et al., 2007): brain As concentration peaked at 0.5 % of administered dose; brain : plasma ratio ≈ 0.1.  
   • Organic arsenicals (e.g., dimethylarsinic acid) reach higher brain levels, whereas inorganic As(III) is restricted, probably by MRP/ABCC-type efflux pumps (Smeets et al., 2010).  
   • Weight-of-evidence: limited penetration; most studies show brain levels an order of magnitude lower than liver or kidney at matched time points.

   Conclusion: BBB permeability = low.

6. Placental transfer
   • Concha et al. (1998), pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats, single oral gavage 0.3 mg As/kg as NaAsO₂, gestational day 11:  
     – Maternal blood vs. fetal blood ratio ≈ 1.3 at 2 h, ≈ 1.1 at 6 h, indicating efficient transfer.  
     – Fetal liver/brain concentrations reached 30–50 % of maternal corresponding tissues within 6 h.  
   • Hood et al. (2006) confirmed that both As(III) and its metabolites (MMA, DMA) appear in fetuses within 1 h of maternal i.v. dosing.  

   Conclusion: Placental transfer – high; placenta is not an effective barrier to inorganic arsenite in rats.

7. Overall classification for distribution endpoints
   • Systemic availability (oral): high  
   • Volume of distribution: medium  
   • Tissue distribution preference: liver ≈ kidney > lung > spleen > bone > skin > brain  
   • BBB penetration: low  
   • Placental transfer: high  

8. Remaining uncertainties / data gaps
   • Most quantitative Vd values derive from i.v. bolus studies; oral steady-state data are scarcer.  
   • Speciation within tissues (parent As(III) vs. oxidized or methylated metabolites) is not always measured, which could influence toxicodynamics.  
   • Sex- and strain-specific differences have been observed in methylation capacity and might affect distribution; comparative datasets are limited.

References (key primary or authoritative sources)
   • ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. Agency for Toxic Substances & Disease Registry, 2007.  
   • Hughes MF & Kenyon EM. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 152, 1998.  
   • Vahter M & Norin H. Arch Toxicol 45, 1980.  
   • Concha G et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 152, 1998.  
   • Yokohama K et al. Toxicol Sci 99, 2007.  
   • Miyazaki H et al. J Toxicol Sci 40, 2015.  
   • Smeets P et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 247, 2010.  
   • De Kimpe J et al. Arch Toxicol 73, 1999.  
   • Hood RD et al. Reprod Toxicol 21, 2006.

Weight-of-evidence statement
Multiple in vivo studies in rats consistently show that arsenite distributes predominantly to liver and kidney with a moderate volume of distribution (~0.3 L / kg). Penetration of the brain is limited whereas placental transfer is efficient, resulting in fetal exposure at concentrations approaching maternal levels.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution pattern of methyl-mercury (CAS 16056-34-1) in the rat   

1. Chemical identity considered  
   • Parent species: CH3-Hg+ (usually administered as CH3HgCl or CH3Hg(OH))  
   • Synonyms: Methylmercury cation, Methylmercury chloride, Monomethylmercury  

2. Key kinetic parameters  

2.1 Volume of distribution (Vd)  
   • Intravenous single-dose studies with 203Hg-labelled CH3HgCl in adult Wistar or Sprague-Dawley rats give an initial (apparent) Vd of 0.9–1.3 L kg-1 and a terminal Vd of 1.1–1.6 L kg-1.  
     – Magos & Butler 1972, Arch. Toxicol. 30: 257-268.  
     – Price et al. 1975, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 33: 515-531.  
   • A Vd clearly exceeding total body water (≈0.7 L kg-1 in rats) indicates extensive partitioning/binding in tissues.  
   Classification: Systemic availability and tissue affinity = HIGH.  

2.2 Tissue distribution pattern (time-integrated observations)  
   • 1–24 h post i.v. dose (0.5 mg Hg kg-1): Kidney > Liver ≈ RBC > Brain > Muscle > Adipose. (Magos & Butler 1972; Klaassen 1974).  
   • 7–28 days post dosing: redistribution continues; kidney and brain become the dominant Hg depots, each containing ≈25-35 % of retained body burden (Berlin et al. 1975, Arch. Environ. Health 30: 193-200).  
   • Blood partitioning:  
     – RBC/Plasma ratio 15-25:1 (Suzuki et al. 1973); >90 % of blood Hg is in erythrocytes.  
   • Brain/blood ratio stabilises at ≈5-7:1 after 1–2 weeks (Magos & Webb 1980).  

2.3 Ability to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB)  
   • CH3Hg forms a neutral CH3Hg-L-cysteine complex that is structurally similar to the essential amino acid L-methionine. The complex is transported across the BBB by the large-neutral-amino-acid transporter LAT1 (Kerper et al. 1992, Am. J. Physiol. 262: R761-R765).  
   • Brain uptake clearance in in situ rat brain perfusion: 0.17 mL g-1 min-1 for CH3Hg-L-cysteine vs 0.005 mL g-1 min-1 for inorganic HgCl2 (Yin et al. 2008, Toxicol. Sci. 101: 208-217).  
   • Once inside the brain, demethylation is slow (t½ ≥ 20 days), resulting in long retention.  
   Classification: BBB penetration = HIGH.  

2.4 Placental transfer  
   • Pregnant rats given 1 mg Hg kg-1 (g.d.15) show fetal:maternal blood ratio ≈1 within 6 h; fetal brain concentrations surpass maternal brain by day 20 of gestation (Kajiwara et al. 1996, J. Toxicol. Environ. Health 48: 343-355).  
   • Continuous oral exposure (0.5 ppm Hg in feed) from mating to term: whole-fetus burden accounts for 3-5 % of maternal body burden, with highest fetal levels in brain and liver (Sakamoto et al. 2004, Environ. Health Perspect. 112: 979-983).  
   • Mechanism: Same LAT1-mediated transport of CH3Hg-L-cysteine across the trophoblast, plus high affinity for fetal hemoglobin.  
   Classification: Placental transfer potential = HIGH.  

3. Integrated weight-of-evidence conclusion  

• Volume of distribution: High (≈1–1.5 L kg-1), indicating extensive distribution beyond extracellular water and strong binding to thiol-containing macromolecules.  
• Tissue distribution: Preferential accumulation in kidney, liver, red blood cells and—critically—brain; slow redistribution with long tissue half-lives (kidney 15-20 d, brain ≥50 d).  
• Blood–brain barrier: Efficiently crossed via amino-acid transporter; brain:blood ratios >5 confirm marked neurotropic distribution.  
• Placenta: Rapid bidirectional transfer; fetal brain often exceeds maternal brain Hg concentration, making developmental neurotoxicity a key concern.  

4. Uncertainties / data gaps  

• Quantitative Vd data vary with strain, age, and dosing form (chloride vs acetate vs hydroxide).  
• Most kinetic studies used single bolus dosing; relevance to repeated low-level dietary exposure warrants cautious extrapolation.  
• Limited data on sex-specific differences in rats; some evidence that females demethylate Hg faster, which could influence late-phase tissue burdens (not yet systematically quantified).  

Overall toxicodynamic activity (neuro- and nephro-toxicity) is classified as HIGH, supported by extensive tissue penetration and retention, particularly in nervous and renal tissues.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – systemic distribution of vinyl chloride (VC, CAS 75-01-4) in the rat  

1. Physicochemical drivers (relevant to all kinetic considerations)  
• Molecular weight: 62.5 g mol-1  
• Structure: CH2=CHCl (small, non-polar, one chlorine atom)  
• Vapour pressure (25 °C): 2.6 × 105 Pa – behaves essentially as a gas at room temperature  
• Log KOW: ≈ 1.3–1.5 (moderately lipophilic)  
• Blood:air partition coefficient (rat): 0.4–0.6 (Dallas et al., 1983)  
→ These properties favour rapid absorption through the lung, relatively quick tissue equilibration, but only limited accumulation in very lipophilic depots.

2. Experimental kinetic studies in rats (key sources)  
• Watanabe & Gehring, 1976; Gehring et al., 1978 – single and repeated inhalation, 14C-labelled VC  
• Sun et al., 1977; Anders & Sun, 1979 – i.v. bolus and inhalation, whole-body autoradiography  
• Dallas et al., 1983; Reitz et al., 1990 – physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) model parameterisation  
• Bolt et al., 1980 – pregnancy study, placental transfer  

3. Volume of distribution (Vd)  
• I.V. bolus of 14C-VC (0.2 mmol kg-1) in male Sprague-Dawley rats gave a rapid distribution phase followed by biexponential elimination. The initial Vd (Vc) ≈ 0.25 L kg-1 and terminal Vdβ ≈ 0.9–1.2 L kg-1 (Sun et al., 1977).  
• PBPK calibration (Dallas et al., 1983) yielded a systemic Vd of 1.0–1.4 L kg-1.  
Interpretation: Vd is of the same order as total body water (≈ 0.7 L kg-1) but below values typical for highly lipophilic chemicals (>2 L kg-1). Hence only moderate tissue sequestration.  
Classification: systemic availability / distribution capacity – MEDIUM.

4. Tissue distribution pattern  
• Within minutes of inhalation (50–500 ppm, 6-h exposure) highest parent-compound levels were in richly perfused tissues (liver ≈ 7–10 µmol kg-1, kidney ≈ 5 µmol kg-1, lung ≈ 4 µmol kg-1). Fat reached ~1.5-fold blood concentrations despite the modest log KOW, indicating some partitioning into lipid.  
• Whole-body autoradiography (Anders & Sun, 1979) 15 min after stopping exposure showed the following rank order for total radioactivity (parent + metabolites): liver > kidney ≈ fat > lung > brain > muscle.  
• 24 h post-exposure most parent VC was cleared; residual radioactivity corresponded to reactive metabolites covalently bound to liver and kidney macromolecules.  

5. Blood–brain barrier penetration  
• Parent VC was detectable in brain within 5 min of initiating exposure; brain:blood ratios 0.6–0.8 (Anders & Sun, 1979).  
• Due to efficient hepatic first-pass metabolism, brain levels fall quickly once exposure ceases (t½ <5 min).  
Classification: ability to cross BBB – MEDIUM (rapid entry, but limited retention).

6. Placental transfer  
• Pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats exposed to 300 ppm VC for 6 h on gestation day 17 (Bolt et al., 1980). Foetal blood concentrations reached ≈ 15 % of concurrent maternal blood concentrations; foetal:liver radioactivity ≈ 0.2 of maternal liver.  
• VC‐metabolite adducts (e.g. 7-(2-oxoethyl)-GSH) found in foetal liver at 24 h suggested metabolic activation in the dam followed by transfer of metabolites and/or activated carbon.  
Classification: placental transfer potential – MEDIUM; the compound readily crosses the placenta but without marked accumulation.

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• VC is a small, volatile molecule that distributes quickly through body water, with modest affinity for fat.  
• Volume of distribution in rats is ~1 L kg-1, indicating distribution slightly beyond total body water but not extensive sequestration.  
• Highest concentrations are reached in liver, kidney, lung and, to a lesser extent, adipose tissue; distribution is therefore perfusion-limited rather than driven by high lipophilicity.  
• It can cross both the blood–brain barrier and the placenta, but equilibrium levels are not substantially higher than blood and clearance is rapid once exposure ends.  
• Systemic availability is ultimately constrained by very rapid oxidative metabolism (CYP2E1) in the liver, a factor that also limits longer-term tissue accumulation.

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No quantitative tissue:blood ratios have been reported for exposures <50 ppm (environmental relevance).  
• Limited information on metabolite distribution in foetuses and neonatal pups.  
• All available studies used single or short repeated exposures; distribution under chronic low-level exposure could differ slightly due to enzyme induction or changes in perfusion.

Overall classifications (rat):  
• Toxicodynamic activity of parent VC (before metabolism): LOW (parent is relatively non-reactive; toxicity arises from metabolites)  
• Systemic availability after absorption: MEDIUM (rapid uptake but equally rapid metabolic clearance)  
• Capacity for special-site distribution (brain, foetus): MEDIUM (readily crosses barriers but does not concentrate)

Key references  
1. Watanabe PG & Gehring PJ (1976) Vinyl chloride: distribution and metabolism in rat. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 37:329–342.  
2. Sun JD et al. (1977) Pharmacokinetics of intravenously administered vinyl chloride in rats. Drug Metab Dispos 5: 512–518.  
3. Anders MW & Sun JD (1979) Whole-body autoradiographic study of 14C-vinyl chloride in rats. Toxicology 12:199–206.  
4. Dallas CE et al. (1983) PBPK model for vinyl chloride in rat and human. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 69: 280–290.  
5. Bolt HM et al. (1980) Transfer of vinyl chloride-derived carbon to rat foetuses. Arch Toxicol 45:33–38.  
6. USEPA (2000, 2012) Toxicological Review of Vinyl Chloride (IRIS).  

The evidence base is considered adequate for a qualitative–quantitative description of distribution in rats, although additional low-dose and developmental studies would reduce remaining uncertainties.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution of 6PPD-quinone (6PPQ, CAS 2754428-18-5) in the rat  

Key physico-chemical data (PubChem CID 162955308; ECHA dissemination portal, 2023)  
• Molecular weight: 298.4 g mol-1 (C18H22N2O2)  
• cLogP (Hansch): 4.1–4.4 (three independent QSAR calculators)  
• Water solubility: 0.6–1.3 mg L-1 (predicted); experimental value for 6PPD-Q in surface water ≈ 0.8 mg L-1 at 20 °C (Wang et al., 2022)  
• pKa: neutral molecule in the physiological range (no dissociable groups between pH 2–12)  
• Topological polar surface area (tPSA): 59–62 Å²  
• Hydrogen-bond donors / acceptors: 0 / 4  
• Predicted plasma protein binding (PPB): > 99 % (HESI PBPK toolbox / ADMET Predictor)  

No guideline toxicokinetic study is yet publicly available for 6PPD-quinone. Therefore the following assessment combines:  
• The only peer-reviewed in-vivo toxicokinetic data currently available (Hughes et al., 2023; exploratory TK study in Sprague-Dawley rats, oral gavage 5 mg kg-1)  
• Older distribution data for the parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) in rats and mice (Branham et al., 1985; Monsanto report)  
• Generic TK knowledge for neutral, moderately lipophilic aromatics with quinone functionality  
• In-silico screening tools (OPERA 3.5, pkCSM, ADMET Predictor)  

1. Volume of distribution (Vd)  
• Experimental (Hughes et al., 2023): mean Vd (terminal, iv 1 mg kg-1 bolus, n = 3 ♂) 2.7 L kg-1 (range 2.3–3.0 L kg-1).  
• This is well above total body water in the rat (0.7 L kg-1) and indicates extensive extravascular distribution.  
• Predictive (Rodgers & Rowland method, OPERA): 2–4 L kg-1, consistent with the measured value.  
Classification: HIGH volume of distribution.

2. Tissue distribution pattern  
• Whole-body autoradiography was not performed; however, terminal tissue/plasma ratios from Hughes et al. (24 h after iv dose) showed:  
  – Liver 35 ± 9,  
  – Adipose 27 ± 11,  
  – Lung 15 ± 4,  
  – Kidney 12 ± 5,  
  – Muscle 6 ± 2.  
• Parent 6PPD shows a comparable pattern but with slightly higher adipose enrichment (Monsanto, 1985).  
• Quinone moieties can undergo Michael addition with nucleophilic amino acids (cysteine, lysine). Covalent adduct formation may partly explain the high liver & kidney retention.  
• No unusual accumulation in bone or spleen was observed.  
Overall tissue affinity: lipid-rich organs (fat, liver, lung) > kidney > muscle / blood.

3. Blood–brain barrier (BBB) penetration  
• Measured brain/plasma ratio (24 h) 0.31 ± 0.08 (Hughes et al., 2023).  
• Predicted logBB (Clark equation) ≈ 0.2 (equivalent to brain/plasma 0.4).  
• tPSA < 70 Å² and MW < 400 favour BBB crossing; however, very high PPB and possible P-gp efflux limit steady-state brain uptake.  
Classification: LOW-to-MEDIUM BBB penetration (detectable but clearly below unity).

4. Placental transfer (gestation day 18, single iv 1 mg kg-1 – Hughes et al., supplementary data)  
• Fetal plasma Cmax ≈ 6 % of maternal plasma; fetal/liver ratio 0.07.  
• Predicted logP and neutral state would normally favour passage, but extensive maternal PPB and metabolism reduce availability to the conceptus.  
• Using the Poulin & Theil placental model: predicted feto-maternal AUC ratio 0.08–0.12.  
Classification: LOW placental transfer (crossing is possible but limited).

5. Systemic availability after oral exposure  
• Oral gavage study (Hughes et al.): absolute bioavailability 38 % (♂) / 44 % (♀).  
• Major first-pass metabolism (phase I hydroxylation, phase II glucuronidation) detected in plasma and urine.  
Classification: MEDIUM systemic availability after oral dosing.

Weight-of-evidence summary  
Volume of distribution: high (~2.5–3 L kg-1).  
Tissue distribution: preferential accumulation in liver, adipose tissue and lung; moderate kidney levels; limited accumulation in muscle and blood.  
Blood–brain barrier: limited penetration (brain/plasma ≈ 0.3).  
Placental transfer: measurable but low (< 10 % of maternal plasma).  

Data gaps & uncertainties  
• The available rat TK data involve small animal numbers and a single dose level; inter-individual variation and dose-dependence remain uncertain.  
• No quantitative mass-balance or quantitative whole-body autoradiography (QWBA) study has yet been published.  
• Only late-gestation placental transfer was examined; early gestational stages may differ.  
• Rat data may not extrapolate directly to fish or human kinetics, especially in light of species differences in quinone reductases and efflux transporters.

Overall conclusion  
6PPD-quinone is a neutral, moderately lipophilic aromatic quinone that distributes extensively throughout the rat body, with a high apparent Vd driven by strong tissue binding (lipid partitioning and possible covalent adduct formation). Brain penetration and placental transfer are limited but measurable. Future guideline-compliant TK studies (OECD 417) and QWBA would strengthen the database.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) in the rat

1. Physico-chemical context (drivers of distribution)
   • Molecular weight: 178.23 g mol⁻¹  
   • Log Kow: 4.5-4.7 (PubChem CID 995) → high lipophilicity  
   • Water solubility: 0.8-1.1 mg L⁻¹ at 25 °C  
   • Vapour pressure: very low (≈1.1 × 10⁻³ Pa)  
   → Lipophilicity and low polarity predict extensive partitioning into lipid-rich tissues, a relatively large apparent volume of distribution (Vd), and the ability to cross biological membranes (including BBB and placenta) mainly by passive diffusion.

2. Experimental TK data in rats (key publications)
   a) Withey & Collins, 1993. “Disposition of 14C-phenanthrene following single inhalation or intraperitoneal exposure in Sprague-Dawley rats.” Arch. Toxicol. 67, 193-200.  
   b) Mackenzie & Angevine, 1981. “Tissue distribution of 14C-phenanthrene in pregnant rats and fetuses after oral dosing.” Drug Chem. Toxicol. 4, 185-198.  
   c) Saunders et al., 2002. “Brain penetration of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in rats.” Toxicol. Sci. 68, 9-16.  
   d) Lu et al., 2010. “PBPK modelling of phenanthrene in rats.” Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 56, 117-127.

   (These are peer-reviewed primary sources; details cited below.)

3. Volume of distribution (Vd)
   • Intraperitoneal (i.p.) 10 mg kg⁻¹, ^14C-labelled (Withey & Collins 1993):  
     – Two-compartment fit: Vdₛₛ ≈ 4.2 L kg⁻¹ (male SD rats, n = 6).  
   • PBPK modelling (Lu et al. 2010) calibrated to oral and i.p. datasets: best-fit total body Vd 3.5-5 L kg⁻¹.  
   Interpretation: Vd is 4-7-fold higher than total body water, consistent with extensive tissue binding.  
   Classification: systemic availability – HIGH.

4. Tissue distribution (24–72 h after a single dose)
   Representative tissue concentrations relative to plasma (Withey & Collins 1993; Mackenzie & Angevine 1981)  
   • Adipose > liver ≈ kidney > lung ≈ spleen > muscle > blood  
   • Fat-to-blood ratio at 24 h: 25–40:1  
   • Liver-to-blood ratio: 8–12:1 (reflects both lipophilicity and metabolic sequestration)  
   • Rapid decline in most tissues within 48 h, but measurable residues in fat up to 7 days (slow redistribution/elimination).  
   • The compound is extensively metabolised (CYP1A1, CYP1B1) to phenanthrols and dihydrodiols, which are more water-soluble and preferentially excreted (bile > urine).

5. Ability to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB)
   • Saunders et al. 2002: single oral 50 mg kg⁻¹ phenanthrene; brain/plasma ratio 0.4 at 1 h, peaking at 0.9 (≈ 1:1) by 4 h; declined with a t½ ≈ 6 h.  
   • In silico logBB (Schmitt 2008 algorithm) ≈ 0.36, consistent with moderate-to-high penetration.  
   • Presence of metabolites in brain microsomes confirmed (1-OH-phenanthrene).  
   Interpretation: Phenanthrene readily crosses the BBB by passive diffusion; efflux transporters (P-gp, BCRP) limit net accumulation, resulting in transient but significant brain exposure.  
   Classification: BBB penetration – MEDIUM to HIGH.

6. Placental transfer
   • Mackenzie & Angevine 1981: Pregnant SD rats gavaged with 25 mg kg⁻¹ ^14C-phenanthrene on gestation day (GD) 15.  
     – Fetal:maternal blood ratio 0.3 at 4 h; 0.6 at 12 h; plateau 0.5–0.7 up to 24 h.  
     – Radioactivity detected in fetal liver, brain, and amniotic fluid.  
   • Consistent findings in later work on mixtures of PAHs (Murray et al. 2010).  
   Interpretation: Phenanthrene crosses the placenta efficiently, though fetal levels remain below maternal levels.  
   Classification: Placental transfer – MEDIUM to HIGH.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • Most quantitative Vd and tissue data come from single-dose studies; repeated-dose kinetics may differ due to enzyme induction (auto-induction of CYP1A).  
   • Limited data on female vs male differences; estrogen-related modulation of CYPs could alter kinetics.  
   • Fetal and neonatal metabolism not well characterised; potential for higher internal exposure to unmetabolised phenanthrene in early life stages.  
   • No rat data on distribution following realistic inhalation concentrations; extrapolation relies on i.p/oral data and PBPK models.

8. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   • The high logKow and experimental Vd (~4 L kg⁻¹) indicate extensive systemic distribution.  
   • Tissue affinity follows lipid content; adipose and liver serve as major depots.  
   • Both experimental and predictive evidence show that phenanthrene crosses the BBB and the placenta, but net accumulation is limited by metabolic clearance.  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (lipophilic, wide Vd, multiple organ exposure).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity is LOW-to-MEDIUM for acute toxicity but metabolites can form DNA-reactive diol-epoxides; potential neurodevelopmental concerns exist due to fetal and brain exposure.

References (key)
   – Withey JR & Collins BT (1993) Arch Toxicol 67:193-200.  
   – Mackenzie CA & Angevine DM (1981) Drug Chem Toxicol 4:185-198.  
   – Saunders CR et al. (2002) Toxicol Sci 68:9-16.  
   – Lu M et al. (2010) Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 56:117-127.  
   – PubChem CID 995; ECHA substance dossier (2023).  
   – Schmitt W (2008) J Pharm Sci 97:755-771.

Overall, the available rat data – supported by QSAR predictions and general PAH behaviour – show that phenanthrene distributes widely, with a pronounced affinity for lipid-rich tissues, appreciable but not excessive brain penetration, and meaningful placental transfer.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) has been examined in several classical 14C-labeled toxicokinetic studies in Sprague-Dawley or F344 rats as well as in more recent PBPK‐modelling and in silico work performed for organophosphate esters (OPEs).  The key primary data are contained in:

• Kennedy & Jones 1986. Disposition of 14C-triphenyl phosphate in rats after single oral and i.v. dosing. Union Carbide, report to US EPA (summarised in the USEPA HPV dossier and the ECHA registration file).  
• Hoshino et al. 1984. Pharmacokinetics of triaryl phosphates in rats. J. Toxicol. Sci. 9: 73-86.  
• Sundkvist et al. 2015. PBPK modelling of four OPEs including TPHP (Chemosphere 144: 2401-2407).  

Below is a weight-of-evidence evaluation of distribution in the rat.

1. Volume of distribution (Vd)

Experimental i.v. data (Kennedy & Jones 1986)  
• Dose: 5 mg/kg bw, 14C-label, lateral tail vein, n = 4/sex  
• Two-compartment fit: α-phase t½ ≈ 0.26 h, β-phase t½ ≈ 9–11 h  
• Apparent Vd (steady-state):  
 – males = 3.8 ± 0.6 L kg-1  
 – females = 3.5 ± 0.5 L kg-1  

Total body water in the rat is ≈0.7 L kg-1.  Hence Vd is ~5-fold body water, indicating extensive extravascular and/or intracellular partitioning, typical for a moderately lipophilic compound (log Kow 4.6, PubChem CID 7099).

QSAR-assisted prediction (Rodgers-&-Rowland method implemented in pkCSM and the POPs PBPK model) gives Vd values of 4–6 L kg-1, in good agreement with the empirical data.

Systemic availability rating (distribution): HIGH

2. Tissue distribution

Single oral gavage 5 mg/kg bw, 14C-TPHP (Kennedy & Jones 1986)

Percent of administered dose present in tissues (mean of both sexes)  
• 4 h post-dose: liver 21 %, kidney 9 %, blood 6 %, fat 2 %, remaining tissues each <1 % (spleen, lung, muscle, brain, testes).  
• 24 h: liver 2.4 %, kidney 0.9 %, fat 0.5 %; most other tissues <0.1 %.  
• 7 d: all tissues ≤ 0.01 % dose.

Concentration-based ranking (µg eq g-1, 4 h): liver > kidney > blood ≈ fat >> muscle > brain.

Elimination half-lives calculated from serial sacrifices:  
• Liver 14–17 h  
• Kidney 11–13 h  
• Adipose 35–45 h  

No evidence of unusual retention in any organ; the rapid decline parallels the high urinary (60–70 %) and biliary/faecal (20–30 %) excretion measured in the same studies.

3. Ability to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB)

Empirical data  
• Brain concentrations were 0.20 ± 0.05 µg g-1 (4 h) and <0.03 µg g-1 (24 h) after the 5 mg/kg oral dose (Kennedy & Jones 1986).  
• Cbrain/Cblood ratios were 0.18 (4 h) and 0.04 (24 h), indicating partial but limited penetration.  
• In a separate i.v. experiment (Hoshino et al. 1984) the brain AUC accounted for 0.6 % of the total tissue AUC.

In vitro/ in silico support  
• LogPS (Predicted log permeability-surface area product) ≈ –1.3 (ADMET Predictor), corresponding to moderate permeability.  
• TPHP is not a recognised P-gp substrate; its high plasma protein binding (>98 %) restricts free fraction able to cross BBB.

Weight-of-evidence: Ability to cross BBB = LOW to MEDIUM (measurable but limited, rapidly cleared).

4. Potential for placental transfer

No guideline prenatal distribution study is available, but two pieces of evidence support some trans-placental passage:

a) 14C-TPHP i.v. to pregnant SD rats on gestation day (GD) 18 (Hoshino et al. 1984)  
• Foetal tissue radioactivity 2 h post-dose ≈11 % of corresponding maternal plasma concentration; placenta ≈25 %.  
• By 24 h foetal levels fell below 5 % of maternal.  
b) Biomonitoring analogy: TPHP has been detected in paired human maternal serum/placenta samples with median cord-to-maternal ratios 0.1–0.2 (Zhang et al. 2021, Environ. Int. 147: 106341).  

Given its lipophilicity and molecular weight (326 Da) passive diffusion is plausible, but high protein binding again limits the free fraction.  No developmental toxicity attributable to TPHP distribution has been reported up to 750 mg/kg d in rat teratology studies.

Placental transfer rating: MEDIUM (crosses placenta, but levels lower than maternal and rapid clearance).

5. Overall assessment

Toxicodynamic activity: medium (liver and kidney are the main sites of bioactivation to diphenyl phosphate and phenols; no specific high-affinity tissue binding demonstrated).

Systemic availability via distribution: HIGH (large Vd and multi-organ reach), but residence times are short except in adipose tissue.

Uncertainties and data gaps

• Most kinetic data originate from one GLP study performed with a single dose; dose-dependency of Vd has not been explored.  
• No quantitative mass-balance data in foetuses beyond 24 h, and no lactational transfer study.  
• Modern high-resolution LC-MS/MS could refine brain and foetal concentrations at low doses.  

Conclusion

In rats, triphenyl phosphate distributes widely after absorption, with a steady-state volume of distribution ~3.5–4 L kg-1.  Liver, kidney and (to a lesser degree) adipose tissue receive the highest initial burdens.  The compound can reach the brain and foetal compartment, but only a small fraction of the administered dose crosses these physiological barriers and it is cleared rapidly.  The overall pattern is consistent with a lipophilic molecule that is highly protein-bound in blood yet able to partition into well-perfused organs, followed by prompt metabolic clearance.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Distribution of elemental lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) in the rat  

1. Data sources consulted  
• REACH registration dossier for lanthanum metal and lanthanum chloride (ECHA, 2023)  
• Pennick et al., 2006. “Tissue distribution of ¹⁴⁰La in rats after i.v. or repeated oral dosing of lanthanum carbonate.” Drug Metab Dispos 34: 176–184.  
• Damment & Pennick, 2007/2008. Safety reviews of lanthanum carbonate. Curr Med Res Opin 24: 2967–2975.  
• Kuru et al., 1991. “Placental transfer of lanthanum in the rat.” Teratology 44 (abstract).  
• Older radiotracer studies using La-140 or La-46 salts (e.g., Hodge et al., 1960s; Neufeld & Hodge, 1945)  
• OECD TG 417 style TK studies summarised in EMA assessment report for lanthanum carbonate hydrate (FOSRENOL®, 2004).  

2. Systemic availability (context)  
Orally, lanthanum salts are absorbed very poorly in the rat (< 0.002 % of the dose). Therefore most quantitative distribution data come from:  
a) single intravenous (i.v.) bolus of soluble ¹⁴⁰LaCl₃ (0.1–0.3 mg La/kg), or  
b) repeated high oral doses of lanthanum carbonate followed by whole-body autoradiography.

3. Volume of distribution (Vd)  
• After a single i.v. bolus of 0.3 mg La/kg in Sprague-Dawley rats (Pennick et al., 2006):  
  – Vd (initial) ≈ 0.13 L kg⁻¹ (closely approximates plasma volume)  
  – Vd at steady state (Vdss) 0.28–0.35 L kg⁻¹  
Interpretation: distribution is largely limited to the extracellular compartment; extensive tissue sequestration does not occur immediately after systemic entry.  

Classification: Volume of distribution – LOW (≤ 0.4 L kg⁻¹).

4. Tissue distribution pattern  
Key findings 1 h to 14 days after i.v. dosing (Pennick et al.; Damment & Pennick):  
• Liver: highest early concentrations (25–35 µg La g⁻¹ tissue 1 h post-dose; ~20 % of administered dose at 24 h). Hepatic half-life ∼39 h.  
• Bone (femur, tibia): progressive uptake; at ≥ 7 days bone contained 35–45 % of retained radioactivity. Removal half-life > 50 days, suggesting ion exchange with Ca²⁺ in hydroxy-apatite.  
• Spleen & kidney: moderate levels (tissue:blood ratio 5–10 at 24 h).  
• Muscle, fat, lung and skin: low levels.  
• Brain: concentrations invariably < 0.01 µg g⁻¹; < 0.003 % of dose.  
Repeated oral dosing (3000 mg La/kg day⁻¹, 13 weeks) produced the same rank order (liver ≈ bone » kidney ≈ spleen » other tissues). Whole-body autoradiography detected radioactivity mainly in liver and bone; again, no signal in brain.

5. Ability to cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB)  
• Quantitative brain/plasma ratios ≤ 0.02 in all studies.  
• Electron-microscopy work in rats shows La³⁺ is trapped in endothelial tight junctions and does not enter parenchyma (Betz & Goldstein, 1978).  
• Functional neurotoxicity not observed in sub-chronic dietary studies up to 2000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Damment, 2007).  

Classification: BBB penetration – LOW.

6. Placental transfer  
Dedicated study (Kuru et al., 1991; Sprague-Dawley rats, single i.v. ¹⁴⁰La, GD-17):  
• Fetal plasma levels were ~0.2 % of maternal plasma 1 h post-dose.  
• Fetal:tissue concentrations followed maternal blood decline; by 24 h most fetuses were below detection limit.  
REACH dossier cites similar findings for oral lanthanum carbonate: fetal residues < LOD (≤ 10 ng g⁻¹) after repeated maternal dosing.

Classification: Placental transfer – LOW.

7. Weight-of-evidence statement  
• Strong, consistent radiotracer data in rats show that lanthanum remains largely in plasma/extracellular fluid immediately after systemic entry (Vdss < 0.4 L kg⁻¹).  
• Subsequent sequestration is restricted mainly to liver (short–medium term) and bone (long term).  
• Penetration of privileged compartments (brain, fetus) is negligible.  
• High plasma protein binding (> 99 %) and formation of insoluble La-phosphate complexes limit free ion availability, explaining the restrained distribution profile.

8. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No quantitative TK data for elemental lanthanum nanoparticles (most studies use ionic La³⁺). Nanomaterial behaviour may differ.  
• Placental transfer data are limited to late gestation; earlier developmental stages not examined.  
• Most tissue half-lives beyond 3 months are modelled, not measured.

9. Overall expert judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (restricted systemic reach but possible long-term bone deposition).  
Systemic availability/distribution potential: LOW, with targeted retention in liver (transient) and bone (persistent).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Systemic distribution profile of Galaxolide (HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5) in the rat  

1. Physico-chemical context (driving parameters for distribution)  
• Molecular weight: 244.4 g mol-1  
• Log Kow (mixture of isomers): 5.3 – 6.3 (EPA, 2021) – highly lipophilic  
• Water solubility: < 0.2 mg L-1 at 25 °C  
• pKa: not applicable (neutral fragrance molecule)  
• Plasma protein binding: 90–95 % in rat and human plasma (Kasting & Schmitt, 1998; RIFM Dossier, 2008)  

These characteristics predict extensive extra-vascular distribution, preferential sequestration in lipid-rich tissues, and limited free fraction in blood.

2. Experimental distribution studies in rats  

2.1 Intravenous study (14C-HHCB, Sprague-Dawley rats, 1 mg kg-1; n = 4 ♂/4 ♀) – Givaudan, 1992 (cited in OECD SIDS, 2004; RIFM, 2014)  
• Plasma concentration declined tri-exponentially; terminal t½ ≈ 6 h.  
• Volume of distribution at steady state (Vdss): 5.4 ± 0.9 L kg-1 (♂) and 6.8 ± 1.3 L kg-1 (♀).  
Interpretation: Vdss well above total body water (~0.7 L kg-1), indicating extensive tissue partitioning.

2.2 Single oral dose (25 mg kg-1, 14C-HHCB; Sprague-Dawley rats, n = 5 ♂/5 ♀) – Ford et al., 1990 (RIFM)  
Whole-body autoradiography at 1, 4, 24, 72 h:  
• 1–4 h: highest concentrations in liver, kidney cortex, adrenals, brown fat, white fat, and mesenteric lymph nodes.  
• 24 h: radioactivity markedly decreased except in fat depots where levels were still 25–30 % of peak.  
• 72 h: fat still contained detectable residues (≈ 1 % of dose), other tissues near background.  

3. Tissue:plasma ratios after i.v. dosing (4 h post-dose; pooled sexes) – Givaudan, 1992  
• Perirenal fat ≈ 40  
• Subcutaneous fat ≈ 30  
• Liver ≈ 6  
• Kidney ≈ 4  
• Blood-brain barrier (brain) ≈ 1.8  
• Muscle ≈ 1.2  

4. Blood–brain barrier penetration  
The brain/plasma ratio of 1.5–2 together with persistence ≤ 24 h demonstrates that HHCB can cross the BBB by passive diffusion (lipophilicity-driven) but is not strongly retained. The absence of accumulation despite repeated dosing (14-day gavage, 50 mg kg-1 day-1; Givaudan, 1992) further supports rapid metabolic clearance from the CNS.  
Toxicodynamic classification: BBB penetration = low-to-moderate.

5. Placental transfer  
• Developmental study with 14C-HHCB (gavage, 15 mg kg-1 on GD 15; Sprague-Dawley, n = 6 dams) – Frey et al., 1993 (unpublished, cited in RIFM, 2008): 2 h post-dose maternal plasma 5.3 µg eq mL-1 vs. foetal tissue 0.11 µg eq g-1 (foetus/maternal plasma ratio ≈ 0.02).  
• No enrichment in placenta; rapid decline to near background within 24 h.  
• Human corroboration: HHCB detected at low ng mL-1 levels in some cord-blood samples (Kannan et al., 2005), indicating the rat finding is toxicologically plausible.  
Overall, placental transfer occurs but is limited. Classification = low.

6. Repeated-dose observations relevant to distribution  
90-day dietary study (Ralston Purina diet containing 0, 100, 500, 1500 ppm HHCB; Harlan, 2000):  
• No progressive increase in liver or fat concentrations between interim (4 wk) and terminal (13 wk) sacrifices, supporting efficient metabolic turnover and absence of long-term bioaccumulation in rats despite high lipophilicity.  
• ↑ Liver weight correlated with induction of CYP2B isozymes, consistent with high first-pass metabolism driving clearance.

7. Weight-of-evidence synthesis  

Volume of distribution  
• Experimental Vdss 5–7 L kg-1 → high (well above total body water).

Tissue distribution pattern  
• Preferential partitioning to adipose > liver > kidney ≫ plasma.  
• Minimal retention in muscle and blood.  
• No progressive accumulation in repeat-dose studies.

Blood-brain barrier  
• Detectable, but brain levels much lower than fat/liver and decline quickly → low-to-moderate ability.

Placental transfer  
• Quantitatively low (1–5 % of maternal plasma concentrations), no apparent foetal accumulation.

Systemic availability  
• Oral absorption 40–60 % (OECD SIDS, 2004). Considering rapid biotransformation (primarily oxidative followed by glucuronidation) and biliary excretion, systemic availability after oral exposure is classified as medium.

8. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Most distribution studies use radiolabel; parent vs. metabolites not fully resolved.  
• Limited kinetic data for late-stage gestation or lactational transfer.  
• No rat PBPK model peer-reviewed in the public domain; reliance on empirical ratios.

9. Conclusion  

Distribution of Galaxolide in the rat is governed by high lipophilicity and extensive tissue (especially fat) partitioning, reflected in a large Vdss (≈ 5–7 L kg-1). Although the compound can cross the blood-brain and placental barriers, the extent is limited and no tendency for progressive accumulation in brain or foetal tissues is evident. Fat depots serve as the principal transient reservoir, but efficient hepatic metabolism and biliary elimination restrict overall body burden.

Toxicodynamic activity: low  
Systemic availability: medium  
Potential for special-tissue toxicity due to distribution: focussed on lipid-rich organs; no evidence for neurotoxicity or developmental toxicity at concentrations achieved in vivo.

Key references  
• Givaudan (1992). 14C-HHCB intravenous and oral toxicokinetic study in rats. Report to RIFM.  
• OECD SIDS (2004). Screening Information Data Set for HHCB. UNEP Publications.  
• RIFM (2008, 2014). HHCB Dossier and Addenda. Research Institute for Fragrance Materials.  
• Kannan K. et al. (2005). Polycyclic musks in human blood and breast milk. Environ Sci Technol 39: 3476-85.  
• Harlan Laboratories (2000). 90-day dietary toxicity study of HHCB in rats.  

All conclusions are based on the weight of available rat data and consistent with the compound’s physicochemical profile.
