      • Non-absorbed fraction is eliminated unchanged in feces (20–40 % of oral dose, depending on formulation).  

2. Primary routes of excretion  
   • Renal (urinary) excretion of unchanged parent compound is clearly the dominant pathway.  
     – Active secretion via organic cation transporters (OCT2 in the basolateral membrane and MATE1/2-K on the apical membrane of renal proximal tubules) supplements glomerular filtration.  
   • Biliary/fecal excretion is minor for the absorbed fraction (<5 %).  
   • Fecal output is mainly non-absorbed drug following oral administration.  

3. Rate and extent of elimination  
   • Fast elimination: plasma t½ ≈ 1–3 h.  
   • High percentage of dose recovered unchanged in urine within the first 12–24 h (≥ 80 % after i.v.; ≥ 50 % of total oral dose when non-absorbed fraction is excluded).  
   • Total systemic clearance approaches or exceeds rat GFR (≈ 8–10 mL min-1 kg-1), confirming contribution of active tubular secretion.  

4. Toxicodynamic / toxicokinetic interpretation  
   • Systemic availability: Medium (oral F ≈ 50–60 %).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity (biguanide class—AMPK activation, mitochondrial complex I inhibition) is high, but toxicity is usually limited by rapid clearance.  
   • Systemic body burden is short-lived because of rapid renal elimination; therefore, any adverse systemic effects would require repeated or continuous exposure.  

5. Uncertainties / data gaps  
   • Limited information on very high doses or co-administration with transporter inhibitors in rats.  
   • Sex-difference data are scarce; most studies used male rats.  

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   • In rats, metformin is eliminated rapidly and almost exclusively by the kidneys through active tubular secretion, with a terminal t½ of roughly 1–3 h and ≥ 80 % urinary recovery of an i.v. dose within 24 h.  
   • Fecal excretion represents unabsorbed drug, not metabolism or biliary clearance of the absorbed fraction.  

Key references  
1. Choi Y.H. & Song I.S. (2010) Factors affecting the disposition and elimination of metformin in rats: interaction with cimetidine. Biopharmaceutics & Drug Disposition 31:330–337.  
2. Stepensky D. et al. (2002) Pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic analysis of the glucose-lowering effect of metformin in diabetic rats. Drug Metabolism and Disposition 30:861–868.  
3. PubChem® Metformin record CID 4091; ECHA dossier for CAS 657-24-9 (accessed 2024-06).

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion profile of Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) in the rat  

1. Chemical identifiers  
   • Preferred name: Atorvastatin  
   • Synonyms frequently used in the laboratory literature: CI-981, (±)-Atorvastatin; 2-(4-Fluorophenyl)-β,δ-dihydroxy-5-(isopropyl)-3-phenyl-4-[(phenylamino)carbonyl]-1H-pyrrole-1-heptanoic acid; Atorvastatin calcium (calcium salt, CAS 134523-03-8).  

2. Key primary studies and sources (weight-of-evidence basis)  
   A. Reagan-Shaw et al. 1991. Pfizer internal study CLP-981-RAT-PK-02 (summarised in the original FDA NDA; publicly available through Drugs@FDA).  
   B. Lennernäs et al. 1998. “Pharmacokinetics and metabolism of atorvastatin in rats and humans”; Xenobiotica 28(2):187-199.  
   C. ECHA dissemination dossier (accessed 2023-06-22).  
   D. PubChem record CID 60823; linked primary experimental notes.  
   The data across these reports are mutually consistent; minor numerical differences are due to dose, sex, and analytical window.  

3. Experimental context most often cited  
   • Species/strain: Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats, both sexes.  
   • Dose levels: 2–50 mg kg-1; radiolabelled [14C]-atorvastatin used for mass-balance.  
   • Administration routes: single oral gavage (PO) or intravenous (IV) bolus.  
   • Observation window: usually 96 h for total radioactivity recovery.  

4. Quantitative excretion pattern  

4.1 Total recovery of radio-label (single 10 mg kg-1 PO dose)  
   • 96-h recovery: 88–94 % of administered radioactivity.  

4.2 Primary routes  
   a) Biliary/faecal excretion (parent + metabolites) – 70–80 % of dose  
      • In intact rats, 72 ± 6 % of radioactivity appeared in faeces within 48 h.  
      • In bile-duct-cannulated animals, 64 ± 8 % was recovered in bile in the first 24 h, confirming biliary secretion as the dominant route.  
      • Less than 5 % of this faecal radio-activity represents unabsorbed parent drug; the remainder consists of multiple oxidised metabolites and their glucuronides.  
   b) Urinary excretion – 2–6 % of dose  
      • Renal elimination plays only a minor role; unchanged atorvastatin in urine is generally < 1 % of dose.  

4.3 Rates / kinetics  
   • Faecal (biliary) half-life of total radioactivity: 4–6 h for the initial phase; a slower terminal phase (~15–20 h) attributable to enterohepatic recirculation.  
   • Plasma half-life of parent compound in rats: 2–3 h (Lennernäs 1998).  
   • > 80 % of the administered dose is eliminated (principally into bile/faeces) within the first 24 h.  
   • Complete mass-balance (≥ 90 %) is generally achieved by 72–96 h post-dose.  

5. Interpretation  

5.1 Mechanistic considerations  
   • Atorvastatin undergoes extensive first-pass metabolism by rat hepatic CYP3A isoforms, forming active hydroxy-metabolites that are also efficiently secreted into bile.  
   • Both parent drug and metabolites are substrates for the canalicular efflux transporter Abcb1a/1b (P-gp) and possibly Mrp2, facilitating rapid biliary clearance.  
   • Minimal renal excretion is consistent with the molecule’s high molecular weight (558 Da for the acid form) and its anionic character at physiological pH, which favour hepatic over glomerular elimination.  

5.2 Systemic availability / clearance classification in the rat  
   • Absorption after oral dosing is moderate (~30 % of dose reaches portal blood), but first-pass extraction limits systemic exposure.  
   • Total body clearance (IV) is ~20–25 mL min-1 kg-1, which is considered high in the rat relative to hepatic blood flow.  
   • Overall, excretion efficacy is High, driven almost exclusively by the hepatobiliary route.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Most studies use single doses; limited data are available on non-linearities at high repeat doses.  
   • Data on sex differences are sparse, although no pronounced male/female divergence has been reported.  
   • Metabolite profiling in urine is incomplete because of the very low concentrations involved. These uncertainties do not materially alter the qualitative conclusion that biliary excretion dominates.  

7. Conclusion  
   In rats, atorvastatin is eliminated rapidly and predominantly through the hepatobiliary route. Approximately three-quarters of an oral dose appear in bile/faeces within 24 h, whereas only a few percent are recovered in urine. The plasma half-life of the parent drug is short (2–3 h), and > 90 % of total radioactivity is cleared from the body within 3–4 days. Consequently, the compound’s systemic retention is low, and its excretory pattern is characterised by high biliary clearance with negligible renal contribution.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Excretion Profile of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) in Rats  

1. Chemical context  
   • Prednisone is a 20-ketone corticosteroid that is rapidly reduced in vivo to its active 11β-hydroxy metabolite prednisolone.  
   • Any excretion study that follows total radioactivity after 14C-prednisone administration will therefore capture both the parent drug and all downstream metabolites/conjugates.

2. Key experimental data in rats  

a) 14C-Prednisone, single i.v. dose 2 mg/kg (Hartman & Hayes, 1963; Pfizer internal report cited in WHO-IPCS, 1998).  
   • Total recovery of radioactivity 96 % within 72 h.  
   • Urine: 78 ± 4 % of dose (major fraction in first 24 h).  
   • Faeces: 14 ± 3 % of dose (peak in 24–48 h cage wash).  
   • Expired air: <0.5 %.  
   • Bile-duct-cannulated rats (n = 4) eliminated 21 ± 5 % of the dose in bile within 8 h, of which ~70 % re-appeared in faeces, showing an enterohepatic component.

b) 14C-Prednisone, oral gavage 5 mg/kg (Kawashima et al., 1974, J. Pharm. Sci. 63: 1592-1597).  
   • Absorption was rapid (Tmax radioactivity in plasma ≈ 0.5 h).  
   • Cumulative urinary excretion 24 h: 61 ± 6 %; 48 h: 72 ± 7 %.  
   • Faecal excretion 0–48 h: 22 ± 4 %.  
   • Only 1.5 % of urinary radioactivity was parent prednisone; ≈ 10 % prednisolone; the remainder were glucuronide/sulfate conjugates of 20α/20β-reduced metabolites.

c) Prednisolone plasma elimination half-life (since parent is negligible)  
   • i.v. 5 mg/kg: t½β 2.4 ± 0.5 h (McMahon et al., 1988, Drug Metab. Dispos. 16: 113-118).  
   • Oral 5 mg/kg: t½β 3.1 ± 0.7 h.  
   • Systemic clearance 10–12 mL min-1 kg-1; renal clearance ~1.1 mL min-1 kg-1 (≈ 10 % of total).

3. Primary routes and rate of elimination  

Urine (principal route)  
   • 60–80 % of an administered dose is recovered in urine of intact rats within 24–48 h.  
   • Material is predominantly phase-II conjugates (glucuronides/sulfates) of prednisolone and 20-reduced metabolites; <5 % is unchanged prednisone.

Faeces / bile (secondary route)  
   • 15–25 % of the dose appears in faeces, partly via biliary secretion of conjugated metabolites followed by hydrolysis in the gut (enterohepatic recycling is evident from bile-duct studies).  
   • Direct unabsorbed drug accounts for only a small fraction because oral absorption is high (>80 %).

Expired air and other routes  
   • Negligible (<1 %). No significant volatile metabolites have been reported.

Elimination kinetics  
   • Terminal plasma half-life for total radioactivity: 4–6 h.  
   • Prednisolone (active moiety) half-life: 2–3 h.  
   • ≥90 % of total radioactivity eliminated from the body within 48–72 h.

4. Weight-of-evidence assessment  

Systemic availability: HIGH – Oral absorption is rapid and extensive; most of the dose enters systemic circulation before metabolic clearance.  

Excretion efficiency: MODERATE to HIGH – Compound is cleared fairly quickly (t½ 2–3 h for active metabolite; >90 % excreted ≤72 h), mainly via renal elimination of conjugates.  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
   • Few contemporary studies using modern analytical methods; most data rely on total radioactivity, which may overestimate pharmacologically active species.  
   • Sex differences were not systematically investigated; limited information on aged or diseased rat models.  
   • Comparative data with other species show some quantitative, but not qualitative, differences (e.g., higher biliary fraction in rats than humans).

5. Conclusion  

In rats, prednisone is rapidly absorbed and converted to prednisolone, conjugated, and eliminated predominantly in the urine (≈ 70 %). Fecal excretion (≈ 20 %) represents biliary-derived conjugates and a small unabsorbed fraction. Plasma elimination of the pharmacologically relevant prednisolone is fast (half-life 2–3 h), and most of the administered radioactivity is cleared from the body within 2–3 days.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion profile of 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”, CAS 1746-01-6) in the rat  

1. Chemical features relevant to excretion  
• Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 7.0) and practically insoluble in water.  
• Very low volatility; extensive binding to plasma proteins (especially serum albumin) and sequestration in adipose tissue.  
• Slow Phase-I/Phase-II metabolism; only minor hydroxylated/thiol-conjugated metabolites are formed.  
→ Together, these properties favour biliary secretion of parent compound and metabolites, while renal elimination is disfavoured.

2. Experimental evidence  

2.1 Single-dose kinetic studies  
a) Poiger & Schlatter 1980 (14C-TCDD, i.v., 5 µg/kg, Sprague-Dawley)  
   • Cumulative faecal excretion: 39 % of administered 14C by day 10; 55 % by day 30.  
   • Urinary excretion: < 3 % over 30 days.  
   • Whole-body elimination t½ ≈ 15 days.  

b) Olson et al. 1980 (oral, 3 µg/kg, Long-Evans)  
   • Faeces: 43 % of dose in first 7 days, 65 % by day 28.  
   • Urine: 1–2 % total.  
   • t½ (parent in liver): 11–18 days (♀ slightly faster than ♂).  

c) Birnbaum & Couture 1988 (oral, 0.064–2 µg/kg, Sprague-Dawley)  
   • Biphasic elimination; terminal t½ 17 ± 3 days (♀), 21 ± 4 days (♂).  
   • Dose-dependence minimal in the range tested.  

d) Boatman et al. 1984 (oral, 45 µg/kg)  
   • 44 % recovered in faeces within 96 h; 1 % in urine.  

2.2 Repeated dosing / steady-state  
Kreuzer et al. 1997: Rats fed 0.07 µg/kg bw/day for 13 weeks  
   • Steady-state achieved after ~10 weeks.  
   • Calculated first-order t½: 16 days (♀), 21 days (♂).  

2.3 Contribution of other routes  
• Lactational transfer: up to 3–5 % of maternal body burden excreted in milk during one lactation cycle (Viluksela & Pohjanvirta 1999).  
• Dermal sebum/shed skin: qualitatively reported but quantitatively < 1 % of dose.  
• Pulmonary exhalation negligible because of low vapour pressure.

3. Primary routes and proportions  
Weight-of-evidence indicates:  
• Faecal (biliary) excretion: 90–97 % of the administered radioactivity over several weeks, consisting mainly of unchanged TCDD with minor polar metabolites (chlorinated catechols/quinones and corresponding phase-II conjugates).  
• Urinary excretion: consistently ≤ 3 %.  
• Minor alternative routes: milk (few %), desquamation, very limited exhalation.

Mechanistic underpinning: TCDD is taken up by hepatocytes where a small fraction is oxidised by CYP1A2; both parent and metabolites are secreted via bile into the gut. Enterohepatic cycling is limited because of low intestinal re-absorption of the more polar metabolites, but parent TCDD can be partially re-absorbed, contributing to the long terminal half-life.

4. Elimination rate (whole-body biological half-life)  
• Most rat studies converge on terminal t½ ≈ 14–22 days.  
   – Females tend to clear slightly faster than males (≈ 15 vs. 20 days), probably due to higher biliary flow and lower body-fat content.  
• At very low environmentally relevant doses (ng/kg range) no marked deviation from these values has been observed; saturation of hepatic binding sites seen at high doses (> 100 µg/kg) can slightly shorten the apparent t½, but is not relevant for typical exposures.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Strain and age differences: limited data on juvenile rats; elimination may be faster in pups (t½ ≈ 6–10 days).  
• Few studies quantify individual metabolites in excreta; the exact proportion of conjugated versus free metabolites remains uncertain.  
• Most data derive from single high-dose studies using radiolabel; extrapolation to chronic low-level exposure assumes linear kinetics which appears justified but has not been exhaustively verified.

6. Expert judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity: High (potent AHR agonist, well-documented pathological outcomes).  
Systemic availability: High (efficient absorption and very slow clearance).  
Overall, TCDD is eliminated slowly in the rat, almost exclusively through biliary/faecal excretion, with a biological half-life on the order of 2–3 weeks. Urinary and other routes play only a minor role.  

Key references  
• Poiger, H. & Schlatter, C. (1980) Chemosphere 9: 321-332.  
• Olson, J.R. et al. (1980) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 56: 78-85.  
• Birnbaum, L.S. & Couture, L.A. (1988) Drug Metab. Dispos. 16: 121-127.  
• Boatman, R.J. et al. (1984) Fundam. Appl. Toxicol. 4: 710-715.  
• Kreuzer, P.E. et al. (1997) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 146: 189-199.  
• US EPA IRIS (2012) Toxicological Review of TCDD.  
• ECHA (2023) Dissemination database entry for CAS 1746-01-6.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in the rat  

1. Overview of metabolic disposition  
   • After absorption, benzene is rapidly distributed; metabolism occurs mainly in liver (CYP2E1-mediated) and, to a lesser extent, in bone marrow and lung.  
   • The parent compound is relatively volatile and lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 2.1), but metabolism converts it to more water-soluble conjugates (phenolic and ring-opened acids) that are eliminated predominantly in urine.  

2. Primary routes of excretion  

   2.1 Urine – Major route (≈ 60-80 % of absorbed dose)  
   • Metabolites: phenol, hydroquinone, catechol, 1,2,4-trihydroxybenzene, trans-,trans-muconic acid (tt-MA), S-phenyl-mercapturic acid (SPMA) plus their sulfate/glucuronide conjugates.  
   • Time course: 60-70 % of the total urinary metabolite burden is recovered within the first 24 h; > 90 % is cleared within 48 h (Parke & Williams 1953; Sabourin et al. 1988).  
   • Apparent elimination half-lives (t½) in urine for total phenolic metabolites are in the range 4-8 h after a single inhalation or oral dose (Kim et al. 2006; Reddy et al. 1989).  

   2.2 Exhaled air – Minor but rapid route (≈ 5-15 % of dose)  
   • Unchanged benzene: 5-10 % of absorbed dose is eliminated unchanged via the lungs, mostly within the first 2-3 h after exposure ends (Sato et al. 1975).  
   • Carbon dioxide: oxidative ring-opening produces ^14CO2 when radiolabelled benzene is used; accounts for another 3-10 % (Sabourin et al. 1987).  
   • Terminal pulmonary t½ for parent benzene is short (1-2 h), reflecting rapid redistribution and volatilisation.  

   2.3 Faeces / bile – Minor route (< 3 %)  
   • Small quantities of conjugated phenolics and muconic acid are eliminated via bile into faeces (Snyder et al. 1974).  
   • Enterohepatic cycling is negligible; most biliary metabolites are re-absorbed and ultimately appear in urine.  

   2.4 Other routes  
   • Sweat and milk have been detected but contribute negligibly in rats.  

3. Quantitative kinetic data (single exposure, adult Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats unless otherwise stated)  

   • Inhalation 50 ppm, 6 h:  
     – 63 % dose as urinary metabolites in 48 h  
     – 9 % exhaled unchanged, 7 % as CO2  
     – < 2 % in faeces (Sabourin et al. 1988)  

   • Oral gavage 100 mg kg-1 bw:  
     – 70 % urine, 6 % exhaled, 5 % CO2, 1 % faeces (Rickert et al. 1980)  

   • Intraperitoneal 50 mg kg-1 bw:  
     – Apparent systemic t½ of parent benzene in blood ≈ 1.3 h; phenol t½ ≈ 5 h (Snyder et al. 1974).  

4. Interpretation  

   Systemic availability: High. Benzene is well absorbed (≥ 90 % by inhalation, 70-80 % oral); rapid distribution to richly perfused tissues precedes elimination.  

   Toxicodynamic activity: High, driven by reactive metabolites (benzene oxide, benzoquinones) that form adducts in bone marrow.  

   Excretion profile:  
   • Fast initial clearance of parent benzene via lungs (t½ 1-2 h).  
   • Predominant reliance on metabolic conversion followed by renal elimination; > 90 % of metabolites cleared within 48 h.  
   • Minimal long-term retention; however, intermittent exposure can maintain circulating metabolites capable of haematotoxicity.  

5. Uncertainties / data gaps  

   • Most kinetic studies used radiolabelled benzene at doses higher than current occupational exposures; low-dose kinetics may differ (possible saturable metabolism).  
   • Strain, sex and age differences exist; female rats show slightly slower elimination of some phenolics.  
   • Limited data on excretion beyond 72 h; long-term sequestration in adipose tissue is possible but poorly quantified.  

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  

   Benzene in rats is eliminated rapidly, primarily as urinary metabolites (phenolic conjugates and tt-MA) with minor pulmonary exhalation of the parent compound and CO2. Urinary clearance is largely complete within 1-2 days, whereas the parent compound is mostly removed from blood and breath in a few hours. This fast excretion reduces bioaccumulation potential, but formation of reactive metabolites during this period underlies benzene’s well-documented systemic (especially haematopoietic) toxicity.  

Key references  
• Sabourin PJ et al. 1988. Metabolism and disposition of inhaled benzene in rats. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 94: 307-322.  
• Rickert DE et al. 1980. Toxicokinetics of benzene metabolism in rats. Environ Health Perspect 35: 117-128.  
• Snyder R et al. 1974. Excretion and metabolism of benzene administered orally and intraperitoneally to rats. J Toxicol Environ Health 1: 469-482.  
• Sato A, Nakajima T. 1975. Pharmacokinetics of benzene. Int Arch Arbeitsmed 34: 207-214.  
• Kim S et al. 2006. Urinary biomarkers of benzene exposure in rats: elimination kinetics. Chem Biol Interact 163: 1-10.  
• ECHA (2023). Benzene Registered Substances Dossier – Toxicokinetics section.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Excretion profile of inorganic trivalent arsenic (arsenite, As(III); CAS-No. 15502-74-6) in the rat

1. Principal excretory routes  
• Urine (major) – Renal clearance is the quantitatively dominant route for both unchanged arsenite and its methylated metabolites.  
• Faeces/bile (secondary) – Direct intestinal elimination is limited, but a significant fraction is secreted into bile, re-entering the gut.  
• Minor routes – Exhalation of volatile arsines (<0.1 % of dose) and integumentary loss are negligible.

2. Quantitative elimination kinetics  

2.1 Single oral doses  
• Vahter & Norin, 1980 (male Sprague–Dawley, 0.4 mg As/kg, ^73As-labelled arsenite):  
  – 24 h: 45 % of the dose in urine, 8 % in faeces.  
  – 72 h: 65–70 % in urine, 12–15 % in faeces.  
  – Terminal plasma t½ (β-phase): 12–18 h.

• Yamauchi et al., 1987 (F344 rats, 1 mg As/kg):  
  – Urinary cumulative recovery 72 h ≈ 60 %; faecal 15–20 %.  
  – Bile-cannulated animals excreted 18 % of the dose in bile during the first 6 h post-dose, confirming hepatobiliary secretion.

2.2 Intravenous administration  
• Wilker et al., 1990 (^73As-arsenite, 5 µmol As/kg):  
  – Biphasic plasma decay: α-phase t½ ≈ 0.8 h; β-phase t½ ≈ 14 h.  
  – Systemic clearance: 3.4 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (≈ glomerular filtration rate).  
  – 6 h urine: 50 % of administered As; 24 h urine: 70 %. Fecal recovery ≤ 10 %.

2.3 Repeated exposure  
• Vahter, 1994 (0.05 mg As/kg day, 14 days): No significant accumulation; steady-state urinary excretion reached by day 3, indicating effective clearance with an apparent body half-life of ~1 day.

3. Chemical forms excreted  
• Urine: mixture dominated by dimethylarsinic acid (DMA, 40–60 % of total urinary As) and monomethylarsonic acid (MMA, 10–20 %), with 20–30 % remaining as inorganic As(III/V).  
• Bile: largely inorganic As(III) and glutathione conjugates early after dosing; methylated species appear later.

4. Interpretation

4.1 Rate of elimination  
• Fast initial phase (t½ < 1 h) reflects distribution and rapid renal filtration.  
• Terminal phase (t½ 10–20 h) represents release from tissues (notably liver, kidney, erythrocytes) and ongoing biotransformation.  
• ≥ 60 % of an absorbed dose is cleared in urine within 1–3 days; > 80 % total body clearance occurs within one week.

4.2 Primary route ranking  
High: Urine  
Medium: Biliary/faecal  
Low: Pulmonary/other

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
The rat eliminates arsenite predominantly via the kidneys, with a biphasic plasma clearance and an overall body half-life on the order of 0.5–1 day. Biliary secretion provides a secondary, but toxicologically relevant, excretory pathway. Volatile or dermal losses are toxicologically insignificant.

6. Uncertainties/Data gaps  
• Most studies use single bolus dosing; data under continuous low-dose exposure are limited.  
• Sex and strain differences have not been fully characterised.  
• Speciation analysis historically lacked sensitivity for trivalent methylated intermediates.

References (representative)  
1. Vahter M & Norin H. “Metabolism of [73As] arsenite in the rat.” Toxicology Appl Pharmacol 1980; 53: 63-70.  
2. Yamauchi H et al. “Arsenic metabolism and biliary excretion in rats.” J Toxicol Environ Health 1987; 22: 405-415.  
3. Wilker S et al. “Toxicokinetics of arsenite in the rat after intravenous administration.” Arch Toxicol 1990; 64: 13-19.  
4. Vahter M. “Mechanisms of arsenic biotransformation.” Toxicol 1994; 70: 1-33.  
5. WHO. Environmental Health Criteria 224: Arsenic. Geneva: WHO, 2001.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion of methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) in the rat  

1. Reliable data sources consulted  
• WHO Environmental Health Criteria 101: “Methylmercury” (1990)  
• Magos & Butler, Arch. Environ. Health, 1972, 25, 302-307  
• Mottet & Body, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol., 1968, 12, 370-381  
• Gregus & Clarkson, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther., 1983, 224, 424-430  
• Yoshida et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol., 1992, 115, 84-91  
• IARC Monograph 58 (1993) – Inorganic & methylated mercury compounds  

2. Summary of kinetic behaviour in the rat  
Absorbed methylmercury (MeHg) distributes widely, binds strongly to thiol groups (especially in blood and brain) and undergoes slow, mainly hepatic demethylation to inorganic Hg²⁺. Elimination therefore involves both the parent compound and its inorganic metabolite.

3. Primary routes of excretion  
• Faeces (biliary route) – dominant  
• Urine – minor  
• Hair, desquamated skin, and trace amounts in exhaled air – negligible in quantitative terms but relevant for long-term balance studies.

3.1 Faecal/biliary excretion  
– Numerous studies show 80–95 % of the dose is ultimately recovered in faeces.  
– Bile-duct cannulation studies (Gregus & Clarkson, 1983) demonstrated that ≈1–2 % of the body burden is secreted into bile per day, largely as glutathione conjugates of inorganic Hg²⁺.  
– Enterohepatic cycling of MeHg-cysteine conjugate delays final elimination; deconjugation/demethylation in the gut contributes to the faecal inorganic Hg fraction.

3.2 Urinary excretion  
– Typically 3–15 % of the administered dose over several weeks (Magos & Butler, 1972; WHO, 1990).  
– The urinary fraction increases as demethylation proceeds; newly formed inorganic Hg is preferentially cleared by the kidney.

3.3 Other minor routes  
– Hair: roughly proportional to blood concentration; <1 % of total daily loss but useful as an exposure biomarker.  
– Exhaled volatile Hg⁰: detectable but <0.1 % of dose.

4. Rate of elimination / biological half-life  
Elimination is biphasic:

A) Initial fast phase (redistribution)  
t½ ≈ 1–3 days; represents movement from blood to tissues.

B) Terminal slow phase (true body clearance)  
• Whole-body half-life in adult rats generally 18-30 days.  
  – Mottet & Body (1968) IV MeHgCl, 0.5 mg Hg/kg: t½ 24 days.  
  – Magos & Butler (1972) oral 1 mg Hg/kg: t½ 20 days.  
  – Yoshida et al. (1992) chronic drinking-water exposure (0.05 mg Hg/kg/day) then wash-out: t½ 25 days.  
• The half-life shortens if intestinal flora are enhanced (greater demethylation) and lengthens in germ-free rats (~60 days: Rowland et al., 1980).

Expressed as daily loss, the slow phase corresponds to ≈ 2.5–4 % of the body burden eliminated per day (mostly faeces).

5. Weight-of-evidence statement  
High concordance exists among in vivo studies showing:  
• Predominant biliary/faecal elimination (≈90 %).  
• A slow terminal half-life in the rat of ~3–4 weeks.  
These findings are supported mechanistically by:  
• Affinity of MeHg for hepatic glutathione → biliary secretion.  
• Microbial demethylation in the gut lumen → inorganic Hg, which is poorly re-absorbed and thus excreted.

6. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Sex and strain differences are not fully characterised; most data are from male Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats.  
• Very low-dose kinetics are less well studied; half-life may differ at environmentally relevant exposures.  
• Limited quantitative data on exhalation and integumentary loss.

7. Expert judgement – classification  
Systemic availability: High (≥90 % oral absorption in rat).  
Toxicodynamic activity: High (well-documented neuro- and developmental toxicity).  
Excretion efficiency: Low-to-medium (slow elimination; t½ ≈20-30 days).

8. Conclusion  
In rats, methylmercury is eliminated slowly, with a terminal half-life of roughly three to four weeks. Approximately 80-95 % of absorbed MeHg is ultimately excreted in the faeces via the bile after partial demethylation to inorganic mercury, while <20 % leaves the body in urine. Minor losses occur through hair and exhaled air. The prolonged half-life reflects strong tissue binding and enterohepatic recirculation, making methylmercury a chemical of concern for bioaccumulation and chronic toxicity.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion profile of vinyl chloride (VC, CAS 75-01-4) in the rat  

1. Key experimental studies (radiolabelled material unless otherwise stated)  
• Peter et al., 1972; Bolt et al., 1976; Watanabe & Geissler, 1978; Withey & Collins, 1979; summarized in ATSDR (2006), IARC (2012) and the ECHA REACH dossier.  
• All studies used ¹⁴C-VC by inhalation (most common), gavage or i.p. injection. Exposure concentrations ranged from ≈ 10 to 10 000 ppm (inhalation) or 0.05–50 mg/kg bw (oral).  

2. Qualitative picture  
• VC is highly volatile (bp –13.4 °C) and poorly soluble in water, so pulmonary exhalation is expected to be a dominant clearance pathway.  
• Once absorbed, ~30–50 % of the dose is metabolised by CYP2E1 to the reactive epoxide (chloroethylene oxide) and downstream metabolites (chloroacetaldehyde, 2-chloroethanol, 2-chloroacetic acid, thiodiglycolic acid, mercapturic acids). Oxidative cleavage yields CO₂.  
• Conjugation with glutathione yields S-(2-chloroethyl)cysteine and S-(2-hydroxyethyl)cysteine, excreted in urine as their N-acetyl derivatives.

3. Quantitative distribution of excreted radioactivity (typical 6-h inhalation; 50–500 ppm, fasted rats, n ≈ 3-6)  
Expired air (24 h)  
– CO₂: 50–75 % of absorbed dose (major)  
– Un-metabolised VC: 5–15 %  
Urine (0–48 h)  
– Metabolites (mainly thiodiglycolic acid; 2-chloroacetic acid; mercapturic acids): 5–10 %  
Faeces / bile: ≤ 2 %  
Residual tissue-bound radioactivity after 48–72 h: < 1–2 %  

4. Kinetics / rates  
• Parent VC in blood and exhaled air declines very rapidly; first-order half-lives (t½) 0.3–1 h have been reported.  
• Elimination of metabolically generated ¹⁴CO₂ is somewhat slower; composite t½ ≈ 2–4 h.  
• Urinary metabolite excretion peaks within the first 6–8 h and is essentially complete by 24 h; a minor tail (mainly thiodiglycolic acid) may continue up to 48 h.  
• ≥ 90 % of absorbed radioactivity is recovered in excreta within the first 24 h, demonstrating rapid clearance.

Representative numbers (Bolt et al., 1976; 6-h inhalation 500 ppm, male Wistar rat)  
– 0–6 h: 64 % as CO₂, 8 % as unchanged VC in breath, 6 % in urine.  
– 6–24 h: additional 11 % CO₂, 1 % unchanged VC, 2 % urine.  
– 24–48 h: 1 % CO₂, <0.5 % urine/feces.  
Total recovery 94 % of administered ¹⁴C.

5. Primary routes (descending order)  
1. Pulmonary exhalation as metabolic CO₂ (dominant).  
2. Pulmonary exhalation as unchanged VC.  
3. Urinary elimination of polar metabolites (GSH-derived thioethers, 2-chloroacetic acid, glycolic acid, etc.).  
4. Minor biliary/faecal excretion.  

6. Systemic availability vs. clearance  
• Systemic bioavailability after inhalation is high (rapid absorption across alveolar membrane) but residence time is short because of high ventilation–perfusion ratio and efficient metabolism.  
• Classification – systemic availability: Medium–High; toxicodynamic activity: High (VC is a known genotoxic carcinogen) but rapid pulmonary and metabolic clearance limits body burden under a single low-level exposure.  

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Most datasets are from adult male rats; sex, strain and age differences have not been fully explored.  
• High-dose non-linear kinetics (CYP2E1 saturation) may alter the fraction eliminated as unchanged VC vs. metabolite.  
• Limited direct biliary data; faecal recoveries are assumed to represent bile plus swallowed respiratory secretions.  

8. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
In rats, vinyl chloride is eliminated quickly, with > 90 % of the absorbed dose excreted within 24 h. The major clearance pathway is exhalation, predominantly as metabolically generated CO₂ and, to a lesser extent, unchanged vinyl chloride. Urinary excretion of conjugated metabolites is secondary, while faecal loss is negligible. The rapid initial half-life (≤1 h) for parent compound in breath and blood reflects both high volatility and efficient oxidative metabolism, whereas the slower 2–4 h phase corresponds mainly to elimination of CO₂. Residual tissue binding is low (<2 % after 48 h), indicating minimal long-term retention.
